EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built in across the Southwest heading into the middle part of June, bringing excessive heat conditions to the region. High temperatures in Phoenix officially approached or exceeded 110 degrees between June 8-12th, which is well above the seasonal normal. The hottest temperature during this timeframe occurred on the 11th when the high temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked at 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 36 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.

Similar to the 20th, thunderstorms once again initiated across the northern Arizona high terrain during the afternoon of the 21st, though more focused along the Mogollon Rim compared to the day prior. Increased steering flow allowed for thunderstorms to move off of the Rim and toward the lower elevations. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) helped thunderstorms maintain intensity as they travelled toward the lower deserts. Given the greater steering flow, faster thunderstorm motion helped abate flash flood impacts. Greater shear across northwest Arizona allowed an organized complex of thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours before progressing through southwest Arizona and the Lower Colorado River Valley area, producing severe wind gusts and dense blowing dust into the early morning hours on the 22nd. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yuma Proving Ground Station 33 mesonet recorded a 58 mph gust.

The hottest temperature was 112 at Ajo. Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, Sells, Picacho, and Safford Airport (record) all topped out at 108 degrees.  Tucson International Airport reached 107.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Heavy rain over the Bighorn Fire burn scar resulted in flash flooding in Finger Rock Canyon. Several homes and an assisted living facility were flooded in the Catalina Foothills. Flash flooding also occurred in Nogales and in western Pima County. Large hail and isolated wind damage were observed in parts of the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1.5-2.5 fell in less than 1 hour over the Finger Rock Canyon watershed with 2.29 recorded in 30 minutes at the Mt. Kimball gauge near the headwaters, which was in the severe burn area of the 2020 Bighorn Fire burn scar. Finger Rock Wash rose over 14 feet at a downstream gauge at the mouth of the canyon. Residents of seven homes and 12 people from another which was used as an assisted living facility were evacuated due to as much as 3 feet of water inside the structures.  Most of the structures will be purchased by the county and torn down. Several of the roads in the area were washed out and the wash structure itself was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust resulted in visibility dropping below 1/4 mile near I-8 and Fortuna Road. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility reported at the Phoenix-Mesa Gateway Airport in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure over the western CONUS resulted in excessive heat conditions across the Phoenix area for the end of August. Temperatures at Phoenix Sky Harbor topped out at 111 degrees on the 30th, while low temperatures near 90 degrees provided little overnight relief from the heat. Above normal temperatures continued into the start of September. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 9 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

An anomalously strong ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest persisted into the latter part of July, leading to temperatures in excess of 110 degrees in Phoenix most days during this time. Temperatures during this time period peaked on the 22nd with Phoenix Sky Harbor recording a high of 114 degrees. Overnight temperatures provided little relief from the heat as morning lows remained in the 90s for several days. On the 22nd, the morning low temperature only cooled down to 95 degrees in Phoenix. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 81 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-rich environment was in place on the 24th with PWAT values climbing upwards of 2 across south-central Arizona. A weak disturbance embedded in the flow aided in shower and thunderstorm development to persist out of southeast Arizona and into central portions of the state. Given the amount of moisture in the environment, thunderstorms were able to produce locally heavy rainfall which led to isolated flash flooding in southern Maricopa County. By the afternoon, daytime heating aided in new thunderstorm development across southeast Arizona, which eventually produced a strong outflow traveling northward into Pinal County. The strong outflow boundary generated isolated dense blowing dust in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 238 was closed between Gila Bend and Maricopa due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Crooks Fire started on April 18 and burned a total of 9,402 acers by the end of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crooks Fire started in the mountains about 11 miles south of Prescott on April 18th. The fire grew to around 9,400 acres and burned well into June. The fire was reached 96% containment by May 25th and access restrictions for the area were lifted. The fire was 100% contained by June 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tunnel Fire started Sunday on April 17 around 4:22 PM about 14 miles northeast of Flagstaff within the Schultz Fire scar (2010). The fire burned through 19,088 acres of timber with understory (abundant brush or small trees) and cured grass. The fire was considered contained on June 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tunnel Fire started on April 17 about 14 miles northeast of Flagstaff within the 2010 Schultz Fire scar. The fire eventually jumped Highway 89 and through Sunset Crater National Monument. The fire also burned at least 30 homes. In all, the Tunnel Fire burned through 19,088 acres. The Tunnel Fire was marked fully contained Wednesday, June 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough developed over the Pacific Northwest and dug southward into |the Great Basin on 21 February.  As an initial shortwave moved into Arizona, the pressure gradient increased resulting in a strong upper-level jet developing. Consequently, winds increased over much of northern Arizona during the late morning and into the night. Vertical mixing through around 700-650 mb where the winds initially in the 30-40 kt range. However, as the trough axis draws approached, the low-level winds at the top of the boundary layer increase to around 50-60 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ADOT wind sensor at Two Guns measured a peak wind gust of 62 MPH at 113 PM. Wind gusts exceeded 58 MPH between 1230 PM and 230 PM. The sustained wind was 40 MPH and higher between 1215 PM and 230 PM.  The Winslow ASOS (less than 5 miles into the next zone) measured a peak wind gust of 61 MPH at 215 PM. Peak wind gusts were 58 MPH and higher between 150 PM and 300 PM. The sustained winds were 40 MPH and higher between 1250 PM and 455 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening cold upper low across the Pacific Northwest brought strong westerly gradients over Arizona with persistent southwest winds. The strongest winds were observed over the Eastern Mogollon Rim, White Mountains, and the Little Colorado River Valley in Navajo and Apache counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Show Low Airport ASOS reported high wind criteria between 915 AM and 440 PM MST. The peak wind gust was 61 MPH at 955 AM. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted southeast Arizona for New Years Eve into New Years Day 2022. This produced in excess of 1 foot of snow across the higher elevations of mountain ranges north of Interstate 10. Wind gusts to 35 mph accompanied the snow at times, but no significant blowing or drifting snow was reported. The strongest winds were farther south and east where gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred in showers and thunderstorms. Rain was widespread in valley locations with one to two inches common. Some foothills and mid slope areas received over 3 inches of rainfall, but no significant flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred in Ski Valley where 12-18 inches was observed. Slightly lower in elevation at Summerhaven there was 10-12 inches. This event spanned from 2021 into 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed low pushed through Arizona on the 22nd and generated convective showers, with a few thunderstorms, in the afternoon and evening. A cluster of showers moved through the Phoenix metro area from the southeast in the evening and mixed down strong gradient winds from aloft. Most measured maximum surface wind gusts were around 25-35 mph, however there were observations in the mid 40s, including a 46 mph gust at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport. There reasonably may have been gusts up to 50 mph that were not measured based on some videos and public reports. The strong gusts damaged or destroyed several COVID-19 testing tents across the metro area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Embry Health COVID-19 testing tents were damaged or destroyed across the Phoenix metro area, including a site at the Mesa Community College, due to strong winds. Loose objects were also blown around and the sites had to temporarily shutdown operations. Convective showers generated the strong wind gusts. Radar velocities from the Phoenix radar were upwards of 40 kts in the East Valley and Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport measured a max gust of 46 mph. The number of tents damaged or destroyed is unknown. A media report mentioned there were thousands of dollars worth of equipment damaged or destroyed, but a more specific amount is unknown. There were no injuries reported. Timing was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level high pressure was situated across southern Nevada, with the local area under an northeasterly flow regime. At upper-levels, a disturbance moving northward out northern Baja California resulted in a diffluent flow, favorable for vertical ascent. The moisture and instability parameters were conducive to thunderstorm activity with PWATs 1.5+ inches and MLCAPE values ranging between 1500-2500 J/KG. As it is typical in a northeasterly flow regime, thunderstorms first began to develop along the Mogollon Rim early afternoon and then descended into the lower elevations during the mid to late afternoon hours, affecting the Greater Phoenix area and then tracking southwestward, affecting southwestern Arizona through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility at times along I-10 just west of Quartzsite due to blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the 20th, thunderstorms once again initiated across the northern Arizona high terrain during the afternoon of the 21st, though more focused along the Mogollon Rim compared to the day prior. Increased steering flow allowed for thunderstorms to move off of the Rim and toward the lower elevations. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) helped thunderstorms maintain intensity as they travelled toward the lower deserts. Given the greater steering flow, faster thunderstorm motion helped abate flash flood impacts. Greater shear across northwest Arizona allowed an organized complex of thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours before progressing through southwest Arizona and the Lower Colorado River Valley area, producing severe wind gusts and dense blowing dust into the early morning hours on the 22nd. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was blown into nearby power lines, causing a power pole to come down. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood Control District of Maricopa County Stream Gauge along Apache Trail in Apache Junction broke Alarm Stage at around 1630 MST, with flow peaking at 1.15 feet (38 cfs). This likely caused the intersection of 4th Avenue and Royal Palm Avenue to become impassable. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed flooding on 14th Street south of Carefree Highway, impacting travel on the roadway. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.75-2.00 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000+ J/KG) combined with an approaching upper-level trough to result in an environment favorable for strong convective activity. Thunderstorms first developed across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains during the early to mid afternoon hours and then slowly drifted southwestward towards south-central Arizona. Activity affected portions of Gila County, where significant water flow was observed along the Russell Gulch area. Additional activity moved from the higher terrain down into western portions of the Phoenix metro during the early evening hours, where damaging winds and blowing dust were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A webcam showed Russell Gulch experiencing a flash flood of at least a few feet high. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under an easterly wind flow, subtle disturbances in the upper-levels moved across the state, helping to instigate thunderstorm activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The strongest thunderstorm activity was concentrated from western Maricopa County through most of La Paz and Yuma Counties. Strong instability (SB CAPE of 2000+ J/KG) and high moisture content (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms, which led to instances of damaging wind gusts as well as flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauge at Delaney Wash reached above action stage at 1.90 feet at 1859 MST. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 5 to 7 degrees above normal were experienced across parts of southeast Arizona July 17 and 18. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found outdoors in Tucson on the 17th and for an additional five individuals on the 18th. Of those, four were found in the desert: two on the Tohono O'odham Nation; one in east central Pima County; and one in Cochise County. A fifth individual died in the hospital shortly after being found in their Safford residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot high temperatures around 105 degrees, or 5 degrees above normal, occurred on July 17 and 18, causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found outdoors in Tucson on the 17th and another found in the desert near Three Points on the 18th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A McDonald's sign in Fort Mohave was blown down, landing on and destroying a parked car, and a large awning on a gas station in Bullhead City was uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dust storm conditions caused zero visibility from Red Lake to 13 miles ENE of Dolan Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust occurred at 1516 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona during the evening and overnight hours. Lines of storms produced wind damage near Elfrida and McNeal with severe gusts in Tucson and a dust storm in Pinal County that stopped traffic on Interstate 10.  Heavy rain also produced flash flooding in Nogales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds resulted in a dust storm across south central Pinal County. Traffic on Interstate 10 was halted in both directions due to visibilities less than 100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona during the late afternoon and evening. Storms produced large hail and damaging winds in the Sierra Vista. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in St. David and far eastern Pima County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size fell in Sierra Vista. Hail broke a window in one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona during the late afternoon and evening. Storms produced large hail and damaging winds in the Sierra Vista. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in St. David and far eastern Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two storms impacted Huachuca City with large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Four Corners area and pushed a strong dry cold front across northern Arizona from the north. This brought gusty north to northeast winds across the area.  Some locations experienced very high and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station (ASBSB) at 9969 feet on Agassiz Peak recorded a peak wind gust of 84 MPH at 440 AM. Wind gusts above 58 MPH were recorded sporadically between 1030 PM and 720 AM.  The station (ASBTP) at 11,555 feet reported a peak wind gust of 72 MPH at 420 AM.  Wind gust of 58 MPH and higher were reported between 930 PM and 630 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving through the Four Corners brought high winds to parts of northern Arizona overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported that large ponderosa pine trees were uprooted and snapped off and damaged RVs and the Lodge 5 miles east of Kohls Ranch at 1100 PM.  A utility company reported that power poles were down about 4 miles north-northwest of Kohls Ranch around 1151 PM. Power outages were reported in the communities of Woods Canyon Lake, Young, Christopher Creek, and Forest Lakes. A driver on Highway 260 between Payson and Forest Lakes reported strong winds blowing debris onto roadway. They had to slow down to maintain control. Wind gusts estimated at 55 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving through the Four Corners brought high winds to parts of northern Arizona overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather station at 11,555 feet on Agassiz Peak (ASBTP) reported a peak wind gust of 69 MPH at 1100 PM with frequent winds gust of 58 MPH or higher between 600 PM and 130 AM.  The station at 10767 feet (WX7FGZ-6) had peak wind gusts of 64 MPH between 700 and 715 PM with frequent wind gust of 58 MPH or higher between 445 PM and 1145 PM.  The weather station at 9379 feet (ASBLS-Little Spruce) had a peak wind gust of 64  MPH at midnight.  Wind gust over 58 MPH were observed between 715 PM and 1230 AM. Power outages were reported in parts of Flagstaff to Munds Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northwest across Cochise, Graham, Pinal and northeast Pima counties during the evening. One storm uprooted a tree in downtown Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a tree near 6th St. and 6th Ave. in Tucson. The tree fell on top of a parked car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. In Tucson, power poles and trees were downed and some roads were flooded. Flash flooding also occurred in Nogales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1.5 in 1 hour caused flash flooding and the closure of Camino Verde Rd. near Bilby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Heavy rain over the Bighorn Fire burn scar resulted in flash flooding in Finger Rock Canyon. Several homes and an assisted living facility were flooded in the Catalina Foothills. Flash flooding also occurred in Nogales and in western Pima County. Large hail and isolated wind damage were observed in parts of the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed tree near 5th St. and Freeman Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough of low pressure brought increasing moisture off from off the eastern Pacific marine layer over central Arizona. This brought extensive upslope snow shower activity coverage, especially along and White Mountains. The trough brought very cold air across Arizona (down to -12C at 700 mb) with saturation to at least 500 mb. This made for a very efficient snow process with significant, widespread accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4 inches of snow fell across the Sedona area and 6 inches at Slide Rock State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the 20th, thunderstorms once again initiated across the northern Arizona high terrain during the afternoon of the 21st, though more focused along the Mogollon Rim compared to the day prior. Increased steering flow allowed for thunderstorms to move off of the Rim and toward the lower elevations. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) helped thunderstorms maintain intensity as they travelled toward the lower deserts. Given the greater steering flow, faster thunderstorm motion helped abate flash flood impacts. Greater shear across northwest Arizona allowed an organized complex of thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours before progressing through southwest Arizona and the Lower Colorado River Valley area, producing severe wind gusts and dense blowing dust into the early morning hours on the 22nd. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were reported down near 12th Street and Pacific Avenue. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough developed over the Pacific Northwest and dug southward into |the Great Basin on 21 February.  As an initial shortwave moved into Arizona, the pressure gradient increased resulting in a strong upper-level jet developing. Consequently, winds increased over much of northern Arizona during the late morning and into the night. Vertical mixing through around 700-650 mb where the winds initially in the 30-40 kt range. However, as the trough axis draws approached, the low-level winds at the top of the boundary layer increase to around 50-60 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winslow ASOS (less than 5 miles into the next zone) measured a peak wind gust of 61 MPH at 215 PM. Peak wind gusts were 58 MPH and higher between 150 PM and 300 PM. The sustained winds were 40 MPH and higher between 1250 PM and 455 PM. The wind sensor on State Route 77 (14 Mile Hill) met high wind criteria from  400 to 500 PM MST. The ADOT wind sensor on I-40 at Jackrabbit had a peak wind gust of 59 MPH at    0403 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level high pressure was situated across southern Nevada, with the local area under an northeasterly flow regime. At upper-levels, a disturbance moving northward out northern Baja California resulted in a diffluent flow, favorable for vertical ascent. The moisture and instability parameters were conducive to thunderstorm activity with PWATs 1.5+ inches and MLCAPE values ranging between 1500-2500 J/KG. As it is typical in a northeasterly flow regime, thunderstorms first began to develop along the Mogollon Rim early afternoon and then descended into the lower elevations during the mid to late afternoon hours, affecting the Greater Phoenix area and then tracking southwestward, affecting southwestern Arizona through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 1/4 mile reported near Tacna in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (1.8-2.0) and an unstable environment (MUCAPE 2,000-4,000 J/kg) across south-central and southwest Arizona allowed for convective development to become strong to severe. Thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona before working toward the south into the lower deserts. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts upwards of 60-70 mph along with several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images and videos showed numerous uprooted trees across the Verrado community in Buckeye, blocking numerous streets. Another image showed a block wall blown over in the area. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (SRP19) at Horseshoe Dam measured a 59 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures from the mid 80s to mid 90s were experienced in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona on March 26. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two males found in the desert, one southeast of Green Valley in Pima County and another west of Douglas in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 10 to 15 degrees above normal, near 90 degrees, were experienced in southeast Pima County on March 26 causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be approached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one male found in the desert seven miles southeast of Green Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure with a 594 dm 500 mb high centered near the Four Corners led to slightly above normal temperatures during the beginning of August. Highs at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked at 107 degrees on the 2nd, while lows were in the mid to upper 80s each morning. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 12 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two indirect fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved across the Great Basin bringing strong southwest winds across Cochise, Greenlee and Santa Cruz counties. The winds reduced visibility to between a quarter and a half mile for 3 hours along I-10 from Willcox eastward. The winds also fanned the flames of a wildfire and destroyed two homes in  Cochise County. Farther west, rain fell across Pima and Pinal counties and was followed by temperatures dropping below freezing the morning of February 24. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that hypothermia was the primary cause of death for one male found in the desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 20 to lower 30s were observed during the early morning hours of February 24, and were preceded by a quarter inch of rain that fell on the 23rd. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that hypothermia was the primary cause of death for one adult male in the desert in the Ironwood National Forest northwest of Tucson.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary shortwave trough moved southward from the Intermountain West and combined with a low pressure center already over the Arizona/Mexico border to produce heavy snow in central Apache County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell overnight in Vernon and Concho.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three fatalities (1 direct and 2 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the 20th, thunderstorms once again initiated across the northern Arizona high terrain during the afternoon of the 21st, though more focused along the Mogollon Rim compared to the day prior. Increased steering flow allowed for thunderstorms to move off of the Rim and toward the lower elevations. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) helped thunderstorms maintain intensity as they travelled toward the lower deserts. Given the greater steering flow, faster thunderstorm motion helped abate flash flood impacts. Greater shear across northwest Arizona allowed an organized complex of thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours before progressing through southwest Arizona and the Lower Colorado River Valley area, producing severe wind gusts and dense blowing dust into the early morning hours on the 22nd. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell between two parked vehicles at a condominium at Roosevelt Street and Granite Reef Road. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Heavy rain over the Bighorn Fire burn scar resulted in flash flooding in Finger Rock Canyon. Several homes and an assisted living facility were flooded in the Catalina Foothills. Flash flooding also occurred in Nogales and in western Pima County. Large hail and isolated wind damage were observed in parts of the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1 in less than 1 hour caused streets to become flooded and impassable in eastern portions of Nogales. Morley Ave. was closed at Madison St.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-2 in 90 minutes caused flash flooding of roadways from southeast to north central Tucson. Road closures included Escalante at Atterbury Wash, Harrison and Pantano Wash, Old Spanish Trail and Freeman Road, Bears Path Rd. south of Tanque Verde Road and Rudasill at Pima Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approaching the area from the west along with moderate levels of instability (MLCAPE 1000 J/KG) and unseasonably high moisture content above the 90th percentile (PWAT 1-1.3) resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong thunderstorms. A complex of strong thunderstorm activity developed north and west of Phoenix during the early to mid afternoon hours. This thunderstorm complex resulted in a strong outflow boundary, which surged southward across the Phoenix metro area between 1530MST and 1630MST. This strong outflow boundary produced areas of dense blowing dust as well as damaging winds, with widespread wind damage observed across several portions of the Phoenix area. Winds across the area remained elevated for approximately two hours, making the timing of the damages reported unclear. Other more isolated thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain east of Phoenix during the mid to late afternoon hours, with one  thunderstorm in particular affecting the Gold Canyon area, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Restaurant sign fell over onto three parked vehicles at I-17 and Thomas Rd. Winds were estimated between 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures around 10 degrees above normal were experienced across the lower elevations of southeast Arizona on July 22. The hottest readings ranged from 107 to 112 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures around 10 degrees above normal occurred across the lower elevations of southeast Arizona on July 22, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 5 to 7 degrees above normal were experienced across parts of southeast Arizona July 17 and 18. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found outdoors in Tucson on the 17th and for an additional five individuals on the 18th. Of those, four were found in the desert: two on the Tohono O'odham Nation; one in east central Pima County; and one in Cochise County. A fifth individual died in the hospital shortly after being found in their Safford residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot high temperatures around 105 degrees, or 5 to 7 degrees above normal, occurred on July 18, causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual who dies in the hospital shortly after being found in their Safford home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A main low pressure centered over southeastern Idaho had several shortwaves rotating around the base of the low and cross through Arizona. This brought showers and thunderstorms over portions of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on Havasu creek near the Colorado River rose 6.7 feet in 15 minutes between 1130 and 1145 PM MST. The river gauge is below/downstream from the Village of Supai. The flood damaged all six bridges in and around Supai Village, some of which were a total loss. The main foot and mule trail to the village was damaged and in need of repair. This 8 mile trail is the only way to the village from a road other than by helicopter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tunnel Fire started Sunday on April 17 around 4:22 PM about 14 miles northeast of Flagstaff within the Schultz Fire scar (2010).  The fire burned through 19,088 acres of timber with understory (abundant brush or small trees) and cured grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tunnel Fire started on April 17 about 14 miles northeast of Flagstaff within the 2010 Schultz Fire scar.  The fire eventually jumped Highway 89 and through Sunset Crater National Monument. The fire also burned at least 30 homes. In all,  the Tunnel Fire burned through 19,088 acres. The Tunnel Fire was marked fully contained Wednesday, June 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Raspberry Fire started on June 14 in the Chiricahua Mountains in Cochise County. Thunderstorm wind gusts of 30 mph helped the fire to quickly grow in mountainous terrain to more than 400 acres. The fire became fully contained on June 25, aided by several days of rainfall. A shortwave trough approaching the area from the west along with moderate levels of instability (MLCAPE 1000 J/KG) and unseasonably high moisture content above the 90th percentile (PWAT 1-1.3) resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong thunderstorms. A complex of strong thunderstorm activity developed north and west of Phoenix during the early to mid afternoon hours. This thunderstorm complex resulted in a strong outflow boundary, which surged southward across the Phoenix metro area between 1530MST and 1630MST. This strong outflow boundary produced areas of dense blowing dust as well as damaging winds, with widespread wind damage observed across several portions of the Phoenix area. Other more isolated thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain east of Phoenix during the mid to late afternoon hours, with one  thunderstorm in particular affecting the Gold Canyon area, resulting in flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Contreras Fire started on June 11 in the Baboquivari Mountains on Tohono O'odham tribal lands within Pima County. Gusty winds helped the fire grow rapidly in dry fuels and rugged terrain. Kitt Peak Observatory was evacuated prior to the fire overtaking the area on the 17th. One cabin, a dormitory and 1 shed were destroyed and the road was also damaged. Power poles were destroyed cutting off power to the observatory. Those damage estimates were near $500,000 but do not account for complete assessments of all telescopes and other equipment. The 20 residents of the community of Pan Tak were also temporarily evacuated as a precaution, but no damage occurred there. The fire became fully contained on June 24, aided by significant rainfall on the 23rd.  Fire suppression costs for the 29,482 acre fire tallied more than $13 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cutoff low was situated over southern Arizona, providing forced ascent. Two rounds of convection occurred, with the first round affecting portions of south-central Arizona during the very early morning hours (0000-0400MST) and the second round affecting far southwest Arizona during the early to mid afternoon hours (1300-1600MST). With the help of daytime heating boosting instability values into the moderate to high side (MLCAPE 1500-2000 J/KG), the thunderstorm activity affecting southwestern Arizona became intense with reports of hail as the cutoff low resulted in well-below normal mid-level temperatures of -13C to -14C. Moisture content was also on the high side during the two rounds of convection as the precipitable water values ranged between 1.0-1.4, which is near record values for early October. This resulted in numerous reports of flash flooding, with the first round of convection during the early morning hours generating flash flooding along State Route 238, just to the northeast of Gila Bend, and the second round of convection during the early to mid-afternoon hours generating flash flooding across State Route 95, north of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed evidence of considerable flash flooding over Highway 95 at Castle Dome Mine Road, impeding and slowing traffic. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cutoff low was situated over southern Arizona, providing forced ascent. Two rounds of convection occurred, with the first round affecting portions of south-central Arizona during the very early morning hours (0000-0400MST) and the second round affecting far southwest Arizona during the early to mid afternoon hours (1300-1600MST). With the help of daytime heating boosting instability values into the moderate to high side (MLCAPE 1500-2000 J/KG), the thunderstorm activity affecting southwestern Arizona became intense with reports of hail as the cutoff low resulted in well-below normal mid-level temperatures of -13C to -14C. Moisture content was also on the high side during the two rounds of convection as the precipitable water values ranged between 1.0-1.4, which is near record values for early October. This resulted in numerous reports of flash flooding, with the first round of convection during the early morning hours generating flash flooding along State Route 238, just to the northeast of Gila Bend, and the second round of convection during the early to mid-afternoon hours generating flash flooding across State Route 95, north of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size was reported at the Yuma TARS Aerostat site. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pacific trough moved across the southwestern CONUS. This resulted in an increase in moisture in the middle and upper-levels and thus showers to break out at cloud bases greater than 10,000 feet. However, given that the air at low levels was very dry with dewpoint depressions greater than 50 degrees F, the showers that fell below the cloud base quickly evaporated as it was approaching the surface, resulting in strong winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds produced by virga showers resulted in some damage to an awning in East Mesa in the intersection between East Broadway Road and South Ellsworth Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over Utah moved southward through northern Arizona and then into New|Mexico. A strong cold front along the Utah/Arizona state line produced a significant band of snow with heavy snow falling over much of the Kaibab Plateau and over the Black Mesa north of Hwy 160. This band then slowly shifted southward through the morning of March 10 then accelerated during the afternoon and evening. The main period of snow reached the I-40 corridor including Flagstaff by mid afternoon and continued into the evening, with travel impacted all the way to the New Mexico border. The winds became northerly behind the cold front with enhanced upslope snow along the north side of the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains through the evening and continuing into the early morning hours March 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Chinle Valley with Teec Nos Pos reporting 9.0 inches and 8 miles east of Kayenta reporting 8.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture was plentiful across northern Arizona with surface dewpoints in the 50s to low 60s. Instability was highest along/south of the Mogollon Rim, with mean MUCAPE from HREF at 1000-1500 J/kg in Yavapai County in the afternoon. General storm motion toward was to the east at 10-15 MPH with light low level flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A river gauge on Black Canyon Creek showed a rise from 1.8 feet to 3.4 feet between 205 PM and 321 PM. A video on social media at 342 PM showed water rapidly flowing over Maren Avenue at a low water crossing on Black Canyon Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough approaching the area from the west along with moderate levels of instability (MLCAPE 1000 J/KG) and unseasonably high moisture content above the 90th percentile (PWAT 1-1.3) resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong thunderstorms. A complex of strong thunderstorm activity developed north and west of Phoenix during the early to mid afternoon hours. This thunderstorm complex resulted in a strong outflow boundary, which surged southward across the Phoenix metro area between 1530MST and 1630MST. This strong outflow boundary produced areas of dense blowing dust as well as damaging winds, with widespread wind damage observed across several portions of the Phoenix area. Winds across the area remained elevated for approximately two hours, making the timing of the damages reported unclear. Other more isolated thunderstorm activity developed across the higher terrain east of Phoenix during the mid to late afternoon hours, with one  thunderstorm in particular affecting the Gold Canyon area, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines were reported in the area between Oregon Avenue to Colter Street and between 2nd to 4th street in Phoenix. Winds were estimated between 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

The wash began to flow around 615 PM and ran well past midnight. The wash was raging at around 700 PM. The waterfall from the flood hit the rock layer below and splashed back up in the air more than 30 feet. Several bodies of farm large animals were in the flood.|Radar precipitation images indicated that heavier rain also fell in the Tanner Wash drainage to the east through south of Hot Na Na Wash.  However, no reports from this area were received.

Instability was highest along/south of the Mogollon Rim, with mean MUCAPE from HREF at 1000-1500 J/kg in Yavapai County in the afternoon. General storm motion toward was to the east at 10-15 MPH with light low level flow.

The flood water over topped the Spring Lane bridge over Big Bug Creek in Spring Valley at 130 PM. A video on social media showed water flowing over a low water crossing on Thunderbird Trail in Cordes Junction at 155 PM.  A river gauge on the Agua Fria River just downstream from the confluence with the Big Bug Creek showed a rise from 3.7 feet to 7.9 feet between 200 PM to 300 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated south of the International Border led to a westerly flow aloft, which caused dry air advection. However, there was still sufficient moisture (PWAT around 1.5 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000-2000 J/KG) as well as a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels to result in the development of strong thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwest Arizona. Downdraft CAPE values were also in excess of 1500 J/KG, which led to favorable conditions for some of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pipleline Fire was human caused and detected around 1015 AM on June 12, 2022 about 6 miles north of Flagstaff. It burned through 26,532 acres by the end of July. The location and terrain of the fire set the stage for extensive flash flooding in and around Flagstaff once the rainy season started a few weeks later. The Haywire Fire was detected June 13 at 530 AM 7.5 miles northeast of Doney Park. It burned a total of 5,575 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pipeline Fire was reported on June 12, 2022 approximately 6 miles north of Flagstaff on the southwest flanks of Fremont Peak. Most of the 26,532 acres burned in June before higher humidity and rain arrived. The burned area lead to many flash flood events once rain started to fall during the yearly summer thunderstorm season (North American Monsoon). The fire suppression costs were at least 11 million dollars. Damage to the forest and watershed was much more than that.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Utah caused a flash flood in the Paria River that drains into Arizona and the Colorado River near Lees Ferry. The river gauge near on the Paria River near the Colorado River peaked at 6.8 feet at 600 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video of flash flooding in Antelope Canyon was posted on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona throughout the day on the 15th. Strong southerly flow out ahead of the low pressure system brought in highly anomalous moisture content across the southern half of Arizona, with PWATs surging to 1.1-1.4, which are near record values for mid-October.  Along with the highly anomalous moisture values, moderate to high instability values were also present with MLCAPE rising to 1000-1500 J/KG. Bulk shear values between 25-35 kts were also present. The combination of the highly anomalous moisture content, instability, and shear along with the strong dynamic forcing to the east and north of the low pressure system was a recipe for these showers and thunderstorms to produce flash flooding as well as severe wind gusts and hail. The greatest flash flood impact occurred along portions of western Maricopa County in the area from Buckeye to Tonopah, The greatest damaging wind impact occurred in the Tonopah area, where two rounds of severe thunderstorms, one hour apart, impacted causing widespread power outages due to downed power poles, destroyed two mobile homes, damaged dairy farm structures, and resulted livestock losses. Severe hail was also reported with a storm that moved into the Mesa to Scottsdale area during the overnight period. ||As the area of low pressure moved eastward on the 16th, lingering moisture resulted in the development of more showers and thunderstorms, mainly concentrated across the higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. One isolated storm in particular developed on a open field near Sun Lakes, resulting in the development of a short-lived landspout during the mid-afternoon hours with no damages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed a significant fire broke out on top of the roof of a home in the area of Happy Valley Road and 95th Avenue in Peoria due to lightning. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple mesoscale convective vortices (MCVs) were seen across Arizona during the morning. Clearing skies allowed for daytime heating to add to the already unstable atmosphere.  As a result, there were multiple flash floods across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell just east of Snowflake with 1.22 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure was stationed over eastern Montana with shortwaves rotating around it over northern Arizona. This provided dynamic support to an already moist|environment with numerous showers and thunderstorms. A stronger shortwave moved from the northwest to southcentral portions of Arizona during the afternoon to provide even more lift to an already unstable atmosphere. Some storms became severe with hail and at least one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Arizona Cypress trees blew over from wind with a thunderstorm. The storm also produce d 1.33 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Streamflow Gauge along Winters Wash in Tonopah showed flow going above alarm stage (1.28 feet) between 18:06 and 18:08 MST, with the flow going above the bankfull stage (3.32 feet) at 18:38 MST and peaking at 4.27 feet (1400 cfs) at 19:18 MST. The flow went below bankfull at 22:49 MST and below alarm stage at 01:43 MST on the 16th. Roads intersecting the wash that were affected from the flow along Winters Wash included Indian School Road, located 1.1 miles downstream of the gauge, and along Salome Highway south of I-10 located 4.6 miles downstream of the gauge. Along Indian School Road, there were reports at around 19:37 MST from a trained spotter of many cars stranded and it is unknown whether or not any of the vehicles sustained damage. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Streamflow Gauge along Cruff Wash showed flow going above alarm stage (2.5 feet) between 20:29 and 20:31 MST, reaching a peak flow of 3.25 feet (448 cfs) at 21:13 MST. Flooding along the wash likely occurred at Agua Caliente Road, located about 2 miles downstream. Flow fell below alarm stage between 21:42 and 21:48 MST. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pipleline Fire was human caused and detected around 1015 AM on June 12, 2022 about 6 miles north of Flagstaff. It burned through 26,532 acres by the end of July. The location and terrain of the fire set the stage for extensive flash flooding in and around Flagstaff once the rainy season started a few weeks later. The Haywire Fire was detected June 13 at 530 AM 7.5 miles northeast of Doney Park. It burned a total of 5,575 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haywire Fire started near Haywire Crater the day after the Pipeline Fire exploded. It burned through pine (ponderosa and pinyon), juniper, grass, and brush. Another fire (Double Fire) started near the Double Crater (two vents) a few days later and merged with the Haywire Fire. The Double Fire was either a spot from the other two fires or lightning caused. Once the two fires merged, 5,575 acres burned. The fire continued to burn through July with little additional spread. The topography and soils (cinders) of the Haywire Fire did not substantially increase the treat of post fire flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance moving northward through central Arizona interacted with monsoon moisture already in place to initiate showers and thunderstorms across the area. The timing of peak heating and the arrival of the disturbance created heavy rain in the Page, Arizona area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in X Canyon, Antelope Canyon, and Lower Antelope Canyon (all the same drainage). Standing water remained in Lower Antelope Canyon on July 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with thunderstorms, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Pipeline/Tunnel (2022) and Schultz Fire (2010) scars caused extensive flash flooding northwest through northeast of Flagstaff. Highway 89 northeast of Flagstaff was closed in both directions around mile post 425 around 442 PM MST. The following roads in far northeast Flagstaff were flooded: Copeland Lane, Gunman Road, Vista de Oro, Pioneer Valley Road, and Neptune Drive. Highway 180 northwest of Flagstaff was closed in both directions around MP 218 at 419 PM. The Stevanna Way and Coconino Estates neighborhoods on the northwest side of Flagstaff flooded with apartments, homes, and streets impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought high winds to portions of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds (58 MPH and stronger) hit the upper reaches of Agassiz Peak (11,555 feet) from 520 AM to 1235 PM. The strongest wind gust was 69 MPH at 920 AM.  A weather station at Lowell Observatory measured a peak wind gust of 63 MPH at 1005 AM.  There was a power outage at 951 AM in the Baderville area due to falling tree branches because of the winds. The power was out for at least 4 hours.  High winds damaged the roof at St Francis of Asis School at 1130 AM.  High winds snapped a tree near Thorpe Park at around 1115 AM.  High winds knocked a tree across powerlines and cause a power outage at around 1019 AM near Linda Vista and Adrianne (north Flagstaff).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with thunderstorms, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video of flash flooding three miles north of Fort Defiance was posted on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona throughout the day on the 15th. Strong southerly flow out ahead of the low pressure system brought in highly anomalous moisture content across the southern half of Arizona, with PWATs surging to 1.1-1.4, which are near record values for mid-October.  Along with the highly anomalous moisture values, moderate to high instability values were also present with MLCAPE rising to 1000-1500 J/KG. Bulk shear values between 25-35 kts were also present. The combination of the highly anomalous moisture content, instability, and shear along with the strong dynamic forcing to the east and north of the low pressure system was a recipe for these showers and thunderstorms to produce flash flooding as well as severe wind gusts and hail. The greatest flash flood impact occurred along portions of western Maricopa County in the area from Buckeye to Tonopah, The greatest damaging wind impact occurred in the Tonopah area, where two rounds of severe thunderstorms, one hour apart, impacted causing widespread power outages due to downed power poles, destroyed two mobile homes, damaged dairy farm structures, and resulted livestock losses. Severe hail was also reported with a storm that moved into the Mesa to Scottsdale area during the overnight period. ||As the area of low pressure moved eastward on the 16th, lingering moisture resulted in the development of more showers and thunderstorms, mainly concentrated across the higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. One isolated storm in particular developed on a open field near Sun Lakes, resulting in the development of a short-lived landspout during the mid-afternoon hours with no damages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pictures and videos from the public and trained spotters showed a brief landspout, lasted about 5 minutes, touching down over the open field just south of Sun Lakes. Unknown how strong it was given that it was over an open field, however, based on climatology, it was rated an EF-0. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Streamflow Gauge along Centennial Wash at the Union Pacific Railroad showed flow going above alarm stage (8.5 feet) between 21:53 and 21:56 MST and continuing to rise for several hours more to reach a peak flow of 13 feet (4315 cfs) at 02:06 MST on the 16th. Flooding along the wash likely occurred along the Old US Highway 80, located 6 miles downstream. Flow fell below alarm stage at 13:34 MST on the 16th. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure was stationed over eastern Montana with shortwaves rotating around it over northern Arizona. This provided dynamic support to an already moist|environment with numerous showers and thunderstorms. A stronger shortwave moved from the northwest to southcentral portions of Arizona during the afternoon to provide even more lift to an already unstable atmosphere. Some storms became severe with hail and at least one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail (1 1/4 inch diameter) hail was reported about 7 miles north of Williams just after a tornado swept through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought damaging winds to northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Williams/Clark Memorial Airport reported a peak wind gust of 58 MPH at 1055 PM. The peak wind gust at the Flagstaff Airport ASOS was 61 MPH at 203 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Streamflow Gauge along Waterman Wash at Rainbow Valley Road showed flow going above alarm stage (3.3 feet) between 19:28 and 20:07 MST and continuing to rise for several hours more to reach a peak flow of 7.25 feet (1700 cfs) at 23:38 MST. A truck was swept off Narramore Road due to the strong current from the wash. There was no information received about the occupants that were inside the truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 5 degrees above normal were experienced across parts of southeast Arizona July 18. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for four individuals found in the desert, two on the Tohono O'odham Nation, one in east central Pima County and one in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot high temperatures 100 to 105 degrees, or 5 degrees above normal, occurred on July 18, causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert, one near Sells and another near Queen's Well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 8 miles west-northwest of Williams in the Junipine Estates neighborhood. Ten 10 structures were damaged and or destroyed (homes and outbuildings). One power poll was blown down and the power was out in the entire community. Camping trailers/mobile homes were turn over. Some rooves were blown off houses.

There was also loss of livestock (unknown how many or type). Winds were estimated at around 80 mph. Timing estimated from radar.

Along with the highly anomalous moisture values, moderate to high instability values were also present with MLCAPE rising to 1000-1500 J/KG. Bulk shear values between 25-35 kts were also present. The combination of the highly anomalous moisture content, instability, and shear along with the strong dynamic forcing to the east and north of the low pressure system was a recipe for these showers and thunderstorms to produce flash flooding as well as severe wind gusts and hail. The greatest flash flood impact occurred along portions of western Maricopa County in the area from Buckeye to Tonopah, The greatest damaging wind impact occurred in the Tonopah area, where two rounds of severe thunderstorms, one hour apart, impacted causing widespread power outages due to downed power poles, destroyed two mobile homes, damaged dairy farm structures, and resulted livestock losses. Severe hail was also reported with a storm that moved into the Mesa to Scottsdale area during the overnight period. ||As the area of low pressure moved eastward on the 16th, lingering moisture resulted in the development of more showers and thunderstorms, mainly concentrated across the higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. One isolated storm in particular developed on a open field near Sun Lakes, resulting in the development of a short-lived landspout during the mid-afternoon hours with no damages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported in the area of SR-387 and SR-187 in Sacaton. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Streamflow Gauge along Rainbow Wash reported flow rising to above alarm stage (1.4 feet) between 17:21 and 17:25 MST, exceeding the bankfull stage (4.1 feet) between 18:17 and 18:27 MST and peaking at 4.42 feet (2368 cfs) at around 18:55 MST. Any flooding impacts from the wash included Old US-80 and Patterson Road, located about 5.6 miles downstream. The wash went below bankfull stage at around 19:31 MST, before another rise above the bankfull stage occurred at around 23:02 MST. The flow did not go below action stage until around 00:47 MST on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A big push of monsoon moisture immediately followed the excessive heat event, and stormy conditions persisted into August. Many storms produced flash flooding, and some produced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burro Creek Crossing was closed from Hwy 93 to mile post 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture, light winds, and clear conditions in the morning allowed thunderstorms to produce heavy rain, flash flooding, and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.20 inches of rain south-southeast of Camp Verde.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture brought thunderstorms and flash flooding to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Government Tank drainage and Pipeline/Tunnel fire scars caused flooding along the side of Highway 89 with only  one lane open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and surface cold front triggered lines of showers and isolated thunderstorms in southeast Arizona.  Lightning from one storm damaged a power pole on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a power pole along Route 15 just north of Highway 86. The pole caught fire and caused a power outage on the Tohono O'odham Nation that persisted for three hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure was stationed over eastern Montana with shortwaves rotating around it over northern Arizona. This provided dynamic support to an already moist|environment with numerous showers and thunderstorms. A stronger shortwave moved from the northwest to southcentral portions of Arizona during the afternoon to provide even more lift to an already unstable atmosphere. Some storms became severe with hail and at least one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Colorado River began to rise in Holbrook (LHB) around 300 PM on October 3. This flow started at around 4.9 feet (34 CFS) increased, and peaked around 13.5 feet (3,700 CFS) at 900 AM on October 4. The river gauge near Winslow (LCW) started increasing at 800 AM October 3 (11.75 feet (53 CFS) and rose to 13.4 feet (700 CFS) by 900 AM. The wave from Holbrook arrived around 400 PM and rose to 17 feet/4,700 CFS by 200 AM on October 4. The river reached flood stage (19 feet/ 8,500 feet) by 100 PM. The river at Winslow crested at 19.7 feet/9,000 CFS by 700 PM. The flow crested at the confluence with the Colorado River (LCM) at 700 PM October 9 (4,400 CFS).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Four Springs RAWS reported winds gust of 65 MPH at 1119 PM and 1219 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mogollon Airpark reported a peak wind gust of 60 MPH at 955 PM MST. A Mesonet station at the intersection of Highways 277 and 377 reported a peak wind gust of 65 MPH at 1013 PM. The Limestone Canyon RAWS (QLCA3) had a peak wind gust of 68 MPY at 124 AM. Wind gust of 58 MPH and higher were measured from 1124 PM to 324 AM on October 23.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread power outages from Golden Valley through Bullhead City, Yucca, and Havasu. Ten power poles were knocked down between Bullhead City and Mohave Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built in across the Southwest heading into the middle part of June, bringing excessive heat conditions to the region. High temperatures in Phoenix officially approached or exceeded 110 degrees between June 8-12th, which is well above the seasonal normal. The hottest temperature during this timeframe occurred on the 11th when the high temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked at 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 36 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the latter part of June, the Southwest was under the influence of a strong ridge of high pressure centered over the southern Plains. This allowed temperatures to climb slightly above seasonal normal values between the 21st through the 26th. At Phoenix Sky Harbor, afternoon high temperatures topped out at 108 degrees for several days during this time. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 13 heat-associated fatalities occurred due to this heat event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the end of June, a strong ridge of high pressure set up across the western United States, becoming centered over the Great Basin. This led to temperatures climbing slightly above normal across the Phoenix area, with the afternoon high temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaking at 109 degrees on the 29th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 10 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought snow and lower elevation rain across northern Arizona December 11-13.  Very cold and dry air was left over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grand Canyon Airport had a morning low of -8 F and Tusayan had a low of -2 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built in across the Southwest heading into the middle part of June, bringing excessive heat conditions to the region. High temperatures in Phoenix officially approached or exceeded 110 degrees between June 8-12th, which is well above the seasonal normal. The hottest temperature during this timeframe occurred on the 11th when the high temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked at 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 36 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure was centered over the Central Plains during the middle part of the month with the Desert Southwest feeling its influence. This brought excessive heat conditions to the region with temperatures in Phoenix peaking at 113�� on the 16th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 9 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Winter Storm impacted southeast Arizona, resulting in heavy snow for some of the Sky Islands and even some light snow accumulations for valley cities between 4000 and 5000 feet. The light accumulations caused some districts in Cochise County to delay the start of the school day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About a foot of snow occurred near the summit of Mt. Lemmon at elevations above 8000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds accompanied the passage of an upper level trough. The strongest winds occurred across southwest Cochise County during the morning of December 28, downing power poles in Sierra Vista.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained southwest winds of 40 mph and gusts greater than 60 mph were observed in the Sierra Vista area. KALK Pioneer Field measured a gust to 66 mph at 1039MST and 1109MST while KFHU Libby Field measured a gust to 63 mph at 1038MST. Two power poles were downed along Cherokee Ave. south of Buffalo Soldier Trail.

A trained spotter at around 4,800 feet reported 9.5 inches of new snow by 630 AM on December 13. This is a significant amount for that elevation. Heavy Snow criteria for that elevation is only 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Crooks Fire burned 9,402 acres in the mountains south of Prescott by June 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crooks Fire started in the mountains about 11 miles south of Prescott on April 18th. The fire grew to around 9,400 acres and burned well into June. The fire was reached 96% containment by May 25th and access restrictions for the area were lifted. The fire was 100% contained by June 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A land spout tornado was seen northeast of Prescott Valley on June 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout was seen north of Highway 89 and north of Prescott Valley. Location was estimated. Photos were sent to the office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and flash flooding in and around Clarkdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain (radar estimate) from a thunderstorm caused flash flooding in Clarkdale. Water flowed over sections of Wildhorse Lane and Old Jerome Highway. Police reported mud and rocks on the roadways with both streets barricaded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person died on Lake Mead when a boat capsized in strong winds and rough waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boat with three men aboard capsized in strong winds and rough waves around noon. Two men were rescued, but one drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built in across the Southwest heading into the middle part of June, bringing excessive heat conditions to the region. High temperatures in Phoenix officially approached or exceeded 110 degrees between June 8-12th, which is well above the seasonal normal. The hottest temperature during this timeframe occurred on the 11th when the high temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked at 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 36 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pipleline Fire was human caused and detected around 1015 AM on June 12, 2022 about 6 miles north of Flagstaff. It burned through 26,532 acres by the end of July. The location and terrain of the fire set the stage for extensive flash flooding in and around Flagstaff once the rainy season started a few weeks later. The Haywire Fire was detected June 13 at 530 AM 7.5 miles northeast of Doney Park. It burned a total of 5,575 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haywire Fire started near Haywire Crater the day after the Pipeline Fire exploded. It burned through pine (ponderosa and pinyon), juniper, grass, and brush. Another fire (Double Fire) started near the Double Crater (two vents) a few days later and merged with the Haywire Fire. The Double Fire was either a spot from the other two fires or lightning caused. Once the two fires merged, 5,575 acres burned. Several years of persistent drought has much of northern Arizona in severe or extreme drought. The drought combined with the time of year, has both the live and dead fuels at critically dry levels. Strong winds also pushed the fire northeastward. The fire continued to burn through July with little additional spread.  The topography and soils (cinders) of the Haywire Fire did not substantially increase the treat of post fire flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pipleline Fire was human caused and detected around 1015 AM on June 12, 2022 about 6 miles north of Flagstaff. It burned through 26,532 acres by the end of July. The location and terrain of the fire set the stage for extensive flash flooding in and around Flagstaff once the rainy season started a few weeks later. The Haywire Fire was detected June 13 at 530 AM 7.5 miles northeast of Doney Park. It burned a total of 5,575 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pipeline Fire was reported on June 12, 2022 approximately 6 miles north of Flagstaff on the southwest flanks of Fremont Peak.  Strong southwest winds pushed this fire up and over Fremont Peak, Doyle Peak, eastern portions of Locket Meadow, Sugarloaf Peak, O'Leary Peak, parts of Sunset Crater National Monument,  across Forest Service Roads 420, 556, 418, and across Highway 89. Most of the 26,532 acres burned in June before higher humidity and rain arrived. The burned area lead to many flash flood events once rain started to fall during the yearly summer thunderstorm season (North American Monsoon).   The fire suppression costs were at least 11 million dollars. Damage to the forest and watershed was much more than that.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture and slow mid level winds allowed slow moving thunderstorms to produce heavy rain and flash flooding over and below the Pipeline Fire scar. Flash flooding was also seen in Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded the Milton/Route 66 under the railroad overpass in downtown Flagstaff. Cars were caught in the flood water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Salt River Project Utility Company Mesonet station measured a 64 mph wind gust located south of Happy Valley Road west of Loop 303.

Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several images showed downed power lines and multiple snapped power poles along Cactus Road between 91st Avenue and 83rd Avenue. Most poles were wooden, but there was at least one metal pole that fell down across the road. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of moisture (PWAT 1.5+) and moderate to high instability (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG) led to the development of terrain and diurnally induced thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms first began to develop across portions of southwestern Arizona during the early afternoon hours and thereafter, additional storm development occurred across the higher terrain east of Phoenix. Outflow from storms, particularly across the higher terrain east of Phoenix led to the development of more storms across portions of the Phoenix metro, particularly in the area between Chandler and Queen Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low-water crossing along Highway 72 just northwest of Bouse was impassible due to flooding. No damages or injuries were reported.
