EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos showed visibility dropping to around 1/4 mile along Highway 95 between Avenue 3E and 7E in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo on social media shows part of a destroyed metal carport lodged in the side of a home near the intersection of Gantzel Road and Ocotillo Road. Timing and wind speed estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 5 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced in the western two thirds of Pima County on June 22 and 23. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three individuals found in the desert, one in western Pima County and two on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures nearly 10 degrees above normal occurred on June 23 with low temperatures in the lower 80s, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Ajo topped out at 110 degrees and Organ Pipe National Monument reached 108. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert on the Cabeza Prieta National Wildlife Refuge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wickenburg Police Department reported Vulture Mine Road at Sols Wash was closed due to flooding. The road was reported closed at 2005MST, but flooding impacts likely began around 1950MST based on radar and an upstream streamflow gage. The streamflow gage is roughly 2 miles upstream and measured 1.69 inches in 1 hour ending at 1900MST. Streamflow exceeded alarm stage at 1930MST. The gage peaked at 4.10 feet and 4815 cfs at 2010MST. Flow fell back below alarm stage at 2100MST.  No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed in South-Central Arizona during the afternoon in an environment with modest instability (MUCAPE between 500-1000 J/kg), seasonal moisture (PWAT around 1.2 inches), but high DCAPE around 1000-1500 J/kg. Many of the storms that developed in the dust prone Pinal County produced at least light blowing dust, with one trained spotter report of visibility falling below a quarter of a mile in the Maricopa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported visibility briefly fell below 1/4 mile visibility in blowing dust near the intersection of Parker Rd and Smith Enke Rd in Maricopa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad ridge of high pressure centered over the Southern Plains led to temperatures climbing slightly above normal in Phoenix on the 19th. The high temperature in Phoenix on the 19th topped out at 104 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, one heat-associated fatality occurred on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified ridge of high pressure became situated over the western CONUS, leading to above normal temperatures in Phoenix going into the end of September. Temperatures during this time topped out between 101-105 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 4 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong ridge of high pressure, with 500 mb heights around 594 dm, was parked over the western CONUS for the start of September. This resulted in well above normal temperatures as well as excessive heat conditions at times. Temperatures during this time in Phoenix officially peaked at 110 degrees on the 6th, while daily highs leading up to the 6th were around 108-109 degrees. Overnight temperatures during this time provided little relief from the heat as morning low temperatures only bottomed out in the upper 80s. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 25 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time. One of these fatalities was that of a hiker who died due to the heat after hiking Spur Cross Trail near Cave Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon, 6 hikers were 4 miles in on Spur Cross Trail when they became lost and ran out of water. The hikers' phones were also dead and had to borrow someone else's phone to call for help. After calling for help, first responders were able to rescue the hikers and airlift them off the trail. One hiker was taken to the hospital but did not survive. A Scottsdale Fire Captain said the individual died due to heat exhaustion and heat stroke. The Maricopa County Office of the Medical Examiner listed the primary cause of death as dehydration and heat exposure. Temperatures in the early afternoon were around 103-105 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike at Agua Caliente Park on the northeast side of the Tucson Metro area ignited a blaze that damaged 225 palm trees and 40 other trees. The park remained closed for 60 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong ridge of high pressure, with 500 mb heights around 594 dm, was parked over the western CONUS for the start of September. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused San Rafael Fire started on May 7 near the International Border in Santa Cruz County. The fire caused evacuations of around 80 residences south of State Route 83, but no structures were damaged. The fire consumed 11,620 acres before becoming fully contained on May 15. Fire suppression costs tallied $2.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant boundary layer moisture was present across most of the area, especially across most of south-central Arizona,  due to the rainfall event that occurred during the 3rd and 4th, which resulted in wet soils. The moisture present combined with clearing skies and nearly calm winds led to temperatures to cool enough that near ground-level saturation was reached. This resulted in the development of widespread dense fog with many weather stations as well as the public reporting visibilities of a quarter of a mile or less at times from the evening of the 4th through morning hours of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than 1/4 mile was reported in the area of McDowell Road near Indian Bend Wash from dense fog. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought isolated high winds followed by isolated flooding from heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oatman Hwy was closed from mile marker 36 to mile marker 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of subtropical moisture moved into the region during the 3rd as a result of southwest flow that developed from an upper-level low located along the west coast and an upper-level high pressure along the Gulf coast states. As a result of this moisture surge, precipitable water values rose to between 1.2-1.4 inches across most of central and southern Arizona, resulting in an extended period of light to moderate rain. Waves of rain affected central and southern Arizona from the morning of the 3rd through the early morning hours of 4th. Most of the Phoenix metro area received upwards of an inch while the areas towards the south, into Pinal County, as well as across the higher terrain towards the east received upwards of 2-3 inches. Even though most of the area did not see flooding from this rainfall event, due to the light rainfall rates, elevated flow along several washes in northwestern Pinal County lead to areal flooding, mainly across Arizona City, where there were reports of intersections flooded with stalled cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images from the Pinal County Sheriff Office indicated several low water crossings around Arizona City flooded, resulting in multiple disabled vehicles. Due to the prolonged period of flooding, some school districts around the area closed down the following day. Between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell in the area. The most likely reason for the flooding was the washes that run through the area likely overflowed. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility as low as 300-400 yards was reported in Yuma from dense fog. No accidents or injuries were reported.

The moisture present combined with clearing skies and nearly calm winds led to temperatures to cool enough that near ground-level saturation was reached. This resulted in the development of widespread dense fog with many weather stations as well as the public reporting visibilities of a quarter of a mile or less at times from the evening of the 4th through morning hours of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than 1/4 mile was reported at the Coolidge Municipal Airport from dense fog. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance tracking through the southern half of Arizona along with moderate instability with MUCAPE values greater than 1000 J/kg and 20-30 kts of deep layer shear resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms first began to develop during the late afternoon hours across the higher terrain, in southern Gila County, and then descended towards the lower elevations, affecting the Phoenix metro, during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home weather station measured 65 mph with tiles blown off from the roof of the house. The location of the home was near the intersection of McKellips and Ellsworth Roads. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures in the upper 90s to low 100s were experienced in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona on May 26-27. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for seven individuals. Four were found in the desert, two on the Tohono O'odham Nation, and two in Cochise County. The other three victims were elderly individuals in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 5 to 10 degrees above normal, around 100 degrees, were experienced on the Tohono O'odham Nation on May 26 causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert, one north of San Miguel and another east of Sells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex developed and tracked into south-central Arizona during the early morning hours on the 4th with widespread heavy rainfall activity across the Phoenix area, leading to flash flooding. The thunderstorm complex continued to track westward through southwest Arizona and into the Lower Colorado River Valley through the morning hours while at the same time weakening in the process.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ADOT webcams showed flooding at the I-17 and Greenway Road underpass, making the road impassable. Flooding was also reported at the I-17 and Thunderbird Road underpass. No damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple videos and pictures on social media showed Dreamy Draw Wash flowing across low water crossings near 12th Street and Kaler Drive as well as 14th Street and Desert Park Lane. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE in excess of 1500 J/KG were more than sufficient to lead to the development of thunderstorm activity, first developing across southeast Arizona and then being steered by the southeasterly flow into south-central Arizona. Portions of the East Valley got particularly hit hard between Gilbert and Mesa, where there were several reports of wind damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US-60 at Val Vista was closed due to standing water on the highway. One car stalled in the standing water. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with divergence aloft associated with an easterly wave near the Arizona/New Mexico border promoted thunderstorm initiation across southeast Arizona during the afternoon of the 30th. Outflow boundaries generated from these thunderstorms raced northwest toward south-central Arizona, resulting in a dust storm in Pinal County where multiple instances of dense blowing dust less than 1/4 mile were reported. New thunderstorm development occurred along these boundaries with some thunderstorms becoming severe. Numerous instances of damaging winds were reported across the Phoenix area and down into northwest Pinal County, which included multiple downed trees and power poles. A very moist environment, with PWATs in excess of 1.80 allowed for thunderstorms to become efficient heavy rainfall producers. Rainfall amounts upwards of around 1.50 within an hour were reported during the event. There were multiple reports of flash flooding across northern parts of the Phoenix area, leading to several water rescues and making some roads impassable. Flash flooding also led to water and mud entering some apartments in north Phoenix causing extensive water damage. Isolated flash flooding occurred across Pinal, Gila, and Yuma Counties. No injuries were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed approximately 1-2 ft of water flowing across the road near 12th Street and Hatcher Road. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to about 200 feet was reported along I-10 near the Sacaton Rest Stop in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV situated in western Pima County on the morning of the 25th led to the development of showers/thunderstorms into Maricopa County. Deep monsoonal moisture, with PWATs in excess of 2 across south-central Arizona, allowed for showers/thunderstorms to be efficient rainfall producers leading to isolated flash flooding. A trained spotter near Arcacia Dr and 186th Dr reported 0.50 of rain in 20 minutes. To the south, flash flooding was reported in Waterman Wash, making roads impassable and leading to a water rescue. Additional heavy rainfall in the evening resulted in more isolated flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video on social media showed water flowing across New River Rd, causing the driver taking the video to turn around. A nearby FCDMC at the New River Landfill recorded over 2 of rain in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the Mogollon Rim over to the White Mountains during the afternoon of the 21st. High pressure centered over the northern portion of the state promoted weak east/northeast steering flow, which helped thunderstorms to propagate toward the west/southwest. Sufficient moisture (PWATs around 1.3-1.4) and high instability (CAPE around 1,000-1,500 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms capable of producing locally heavy rainfall across southern Gila County. By early evening, strong to severe thunderstorms pushed into the Globe area producing damaging winds, hail, and flash flooding in the area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Videos show Pinal Creek overtopping a bridge along Six Shooter Canyon Road near Delores Lane making the road impassable. Flood waters from the overtopped bridge are seen in a video traveling down Six Shooter Canyon Road. A rain gage upstream recorded 1.1 of rain in 15 minutes. No injuries or damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough moving westward near the Arizona/New Mexico border aided in thunderstorm development during the afternoon of the 27th across far eastern Arizona. Thunderstorms progressed toward the west before reaching southern Gila County late in the afternoon. Deep monsoonal moisture, with PWATs in excess of 1.70, resulted in thunderstorms being efficient rainfall producers. In the Globe area, rainfall amounts upwards of 1.70 in an hour were reported. Heavy rainfall in the area led to isolated flash flooding near the San Carlos Apache Indian Reservation line along with several reports of minor flooding in the Globe area. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Videos on social media showed high flow in a wash near the San Carlos Apache Indian Reservation line between Globe and San Carlos, making low water crossings through the wash impassable. Further downstream, a 5 ft rise was recorded in Gilson Wash. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the Mogollon Rim over to the White Mountains during the afternoon of the 21st. High pressure centered over the northern portion of the state promoted weak east/northeast steering flow, which helped thunderstorms to propagate toward the west/southwest. Sufficient moisture (PWATs around 1.3-1.4) and high instability (CAPE around 1,000-1,500 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms capable of producing locally heavy rainfall across southern Gila County. By early evening, strong to severe thunderstorms pushed into the Globe area producing damaging winds, hail, and flash flooding in the area. No injuries were reported.

The rapid increase in flow in Pinal Creek made Beer Tree Crossing impassable, with images showing barricades in place to close the road. A nearby stream gage observed a 1.84 ft increase in flow in Pinal Creek. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with divergence aloft associated with an easterly wave near the Arizona/New Mexico border promoted thunderstorm initiation across southeast Arizona during the afternoon of the 30th. Outflow boundaries generated from these thunderstorms raced northwest toward south-central Arizona, resulting in a dust storm in Pinal County where multiple instances of dense blowing dust less than 1/4 mile were reported. New thunderstorm development occurred along these boundaries with some thunderstorms becoming severe. Numerous instances of damaging winds were reported across the Phoenix area and down into northwest Pinal County, which included multiple downed trees and power poles. A very moist environment, with PWATs in excess of 1.80 allowed for thunderstorms to become efficient heavy rainfall producers. Rainfall amounts upwards of around 1.50 within an hour were reported during the event. There were multiple reports of flash flooding across northern parts of the Phoenix area, leading to several water rescues and making some roads impassable. Flash flooding also led to water and mud entering some apartments in north Phoenix causing extensive water damage. Isolated flash flooding occurred across Pinal, Gila, and Yuma Counties. No injuries were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 ft of water and mud entered some first floor apartments at Central Avenue and Bell Road after the drainage system was overcome. Some cars in the parking lot of the apartment were also damaged. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough moving westward near the Arizona/New Mexico border aided in thunderstorm development during the afternoon of the 27th across far eastern Arizona. Thunderstorms progressed toward the west before reaching southern Gila County late in the afternoon. Deep monsoonal moisture, with PWATs in excess of 1.70, resulted in thunderstorms being efficient rainfall producers. In the Globe area, rainfall amounts upwards of 1.70 in an hour were reported. Heavy rainfall in the area led to isolated flash flooding near the San Carlos Apache Indian Reservation line along with several reports of minor flooding in the Globe area. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of rapid-moving floodwaters through a wash through Waggin' Vineyard and report that waves reached nearly 10 feet at times. No damages or injuries were reported. Timing based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of near record moisture content (PWATs 1.8-2.0 inches), moderate to strong instability (SBCAPE  2000 J/KG), very slow steering flow and an upper-level disturbance resulted in a very conducive environment for the generation of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona, resulting in flash flooding. Activity first developed across the foothills and higher terrain of Eastern Maricopa and Gila Counties during the late morning-early afternoon hours. Another area of thunderstorms developed over northwestern Arizona during the mid-late afternoon hours and move southward towards La Paz County into the evening hours. One of the areas severely hit by flash flooding was Apache Junction, AZ. Weekes Wash, which runs through the town, went of out of its banks, causing widespread flooding impacts across the city. Flooding also took place from drainage that occurred off the Goldfield and Superstitions Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Arizona Department of Transportation reported SR 88 was closed due to flooding between milepost 201 and 205. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video from a residence in Arizona City shows visibility down to a couple hundred feet in dense dust. No damages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms initially began across parts of southern Gila County, along with eastern Maricopa County and northeast Pinal County, producing locally heavy rainfall leading to isolated flash flooding. By the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 20th, new thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) aided in maintaining strong to severe thunderstorms into the Phoenix area as thunderstorms pushed toward the south and into the lower deserts during the evening. DCAPE around 1,000-1,200 J/kg promoted strong to severe downbursts with the strongest thunderstorms. Meanwhile, PWATs around 1.7-1.9 aided in thunderstorms being heavy rainfall producers, leading to several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Videos showed considerable flow in Bloody Tanks Wash, impacting Schulze Ranch Road. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (1.8-2.0) and an unstable environment (MUCAPE 2,000-4,000 J/kg) across south-central and southwest Arizona allowed for convective development to become strong to severe. Thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona before working toward the south into the lower deserts. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts upwards of 60-70 mph along with several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust reduced visibility below 1/4 mile near Magma Road and Hunt Highway. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to be a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southwest Arizona. A cluster of thunderstorms developed near Yuma during the mid-afternoon hours leading to flash flooding and damaging winds across the Fortuna Hills area. Thereafter, additional storm complexes developed during the late afternoon and evening hours across portions of Yuma and La Paz Counties, likely due to the combination of terrain lift as well as colliding outflows from the storms that developed earlier in the afternoon across portions of Imperial and Riverside Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US-95 near Milepost 69 was flooded. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of moisture (PWAT 1.5+) and moderate to high instability (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG) led to the development of terrain and diurnally induced thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms first began to develop across portions of southwestern Arizona during the early afternoon hours and thereafter, additional storm development occurred across the higher terrain east of Phoenix. Outflow from storms, particularly across the higher terrain east of Phoenix led to the development of more storms across portions of the Phoenix metro, particularly in the area between Chandler and Queen Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from trained spotter described window broken from north side of the local post office and roof damage at LNWilliams Engineering and Mining Manufacturing Building. Both of these buildings located along US-60 in the town of Salome. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of moisture (PWAT 1.5+) and moderate to high instability (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG) led to the development of terrain and diurnally induced thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms first began to develop across portions of southwestern Arizona during the early afternoon hours and thereafter, additional storm development occurred across the higher terrain east of Phoenix. Outflow from storms, particularly across the higher terrain east of Phoenix led to the development of more storms across portions of the Phoenix metro, particularly in the area between Chandler and Queen Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 mph wind gust was measured at the Chandler Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than 1/4 mile reported along I-8 near Stanfield in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms across south-central Arizona on the 14th. Daytime heating aided in thunderstorm development across the high terrain north of Phoenix before sending outflows traveling toward the south into the lower elevations. New thunderstorm development occurred along these outflows into the Valley where they produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, and isolated flash flooding. Additional thunderstorm development occurred across southwest Arizona where better moisture allowed thunderstorms to be efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images showed a large tree toppled and leaning onto a home near Ocotillo Road and Arizona Avenue. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms across south-central Arizona on the 14th. Daytime heating aided in thunderstorm development across the high terrain north of Phoenix before sending outflows traveling toward the south into the lower elevations. New thunderstorm development occurred along these outflows into the Valley where they produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, and isolated flash flooding. Additional thunderstorm development occurred across southwest Arizona where better moisture allowed thunderstorms to be efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of wind damage in Anthem at Merrill Ranch, including 35 plus trees uprooted, snapped, or with branches down. There were also reports of roof tiles blown off and a Gazebo that was lifted from one yard to another. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance tracking through the southern half of Arizona along with moderate instability with MUCAPE values greater than 1000 J/kg and 20-30 kts of deep layer shear resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms first began to develop during the late afternoon hours across the higher terrain, in southern Gila County, and then descended towards the lower elevations, affecting the Phoenix metro, during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images taken from storm chaser passing through the area showed power lines down as well as a destroyed metal shed on Harquahala Valley Road just north of West Bethany Home Road west of Tonopah. Winds were estimated at 60 mph. Timing was estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level high pressure was situated across southern Nevada, with the local area under an northeasterly flow regime. At upper-levels, a disturbance moving northward out northern Baja California resulted in a diffluent flow, favorable for vertical ascent. The moisture and instability parameters were conducive to thunderstorm activity with PWATs 1.5+ inches and MLCAPE values ranging between 1500-2500 J/KG. As it is typical in a northeasterly flow regime, thunderstorms first began to develop along the Mogollon Rim early afternoon and then descended into the lower elevations during the mid to late afternoon hours, affecting the Greater Phoenix area and then tracking southwestward, affecting southwestern Arizona through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media video shows a large tree was toppled and damaged some concrete and metal fencing at a residence near Peoria Ave. and 46th Ave. Winds were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.

Isolated showers and thunderstorms initially began across parts of southern Gila County, along with eastern Maricopa County and northeast Pinal County, producing locally heavy rainfall leading to isolated flash flooding. By the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 20th, new thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) aided in maintaining strong to severe thunderstorms into the Phoenix area as thunderstorms pushed toward the south and into the lower deserts during the evening. DCAPE around 1,000-1,200 J/kg promoted strong to severe downbursts with the strongest thunderstorms. Meanwhile, PWATs around 1.7-1.9 aided in thunderstorms being heavy rainfall producers, leading to several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed a wash flowing across a low water crossing along Silver King Road, making the road impassable. Other videos in the Queen Valley area also showed high flow in Queen Creek. Timing estimated from radar. No damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: ADOT reported that SR 95 was closed due to flooding near milepost 80. There were also reports of a couple of cars that attempted to cross the flooded road but were not able to make it across and had to be pulled out. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images showed several trees and branches down at an apartment complex near 43rd Avenue and Bell Road. A couple of carports were damaged from the downed trees. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Mogollon Rim to the Valley (Rim-to-Valley), with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada, a multicell storm cluster descended out of the White Mountains, through Gila County, and into the lower deserts of Maricopa and Pinal Counties in the early overnight hours of the 1st. Moisture levels were near average for the time of year, but steep mid level lapse rates over the high terrain (7-8 C/km) and modest shear (Sfc-6km around 20-25 kts) was sufficient to support the development of an organized multicell storm cluster. The environment was also supportive of strong downbursts, with DCAPE up around 1000-1500 J/kg. A single ordinary storm also developed in the Queen Creek/San Tan Valley area a couple hours before the multicell cluster moved into the lower deserts, and produced a damaging downburst. Strong winds leading to a dust storm was eventually generated by the multicell cluster once it moved into Maricopa and Pinal Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility fell to 200 feet near Queen Creek Road and Sossaman Road due to dense blowing dust. No injuries or major traffic disruptions were reported.

Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a home was struck by lightning near Hunt Highway and Peace Pipe Place. The home caught fire and was fully destroyed. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Signal Butte Road between East Germann Road and East Barnes Parkway as well as Germann Road at South Crismon Road were closed due flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large downed trees, including one crashing through a cinderblock wall and another that fell toward a house along East 212th St and East Village Loop North in Queen Creek. Timing estimated from radar. Estimated wind gusts 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station at Horseshoe Dam measured a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from trained spotter as well as videos from the broadcast media showed about a one-mile stretch of power poles blown over West Battaglia Drive between Sunland Gin and Overfield Roads. Thousands of people were left without power. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE in excess of 1500 J/KG were more than sufficient to lead to the development of thunderstorm activity, first developing across southeast Arizona and then being steered by the southeasterly flow into south-central Arizona. Portions of the East Valley got particularly hit hard between Gilbert and Mesa, where there were several reports of wind damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Image taken by the Salt River Project Utility Company showed a snapped power pole along the northeast corner of N Lindsay and E Guadalupe Rd. Winds were estimated at 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.75-2.00 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000+ J/KG) combined with an approaching upper-level trough to result in an environment favorable for strong convective activity. Thunderstorms first developed across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains during the early to mid afternoon hours and then slowly drifted southwestward towards south-central Arizona. Activity affected portions of Gila County, where significant water flow was observed along the Russell Gulch area. Additional activity moved from the higher terrain down into western portions of the Phoenix metro during the early evening hours, where damaging winds and blowing dust were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility less than 1/4 mile along Loop 303 just south of Surprise in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR-87 was closed between Loop-202 and Shea Blvd due to widespread wind damage. One mobile home and one modular home were destroyed resulting in one injury, requiring the person to be transported to the hospital and be placed in the Intensive Care Unit. Numerous power poles were blown down as well as several transmission towers that had collapsed. The transmission towers were in an area west of Gilbert Rd and along Highway 87. Salt River Project (SRP) utilities confirmed a total of 16 power poles were either blown over or snapped. They also confirmed another 5 (230 volt) transmission towers had collapsed. Other less severe damage was widespread across the region that included shingles blown off numerous homes, tree damage and damage to road signs. 20 other homes were listed as inhabitable. Initial wind estimates from NWS Phoenix were 80-100 mph. SRP rated their transmission towers for 90-95 mph. So, speeds may have actually been 100+ mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated south of the International Border led to a westerly flow aloft, which caused dry air advection. However, there was still sufficient moisture (PWAT around 1.5 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000-2000 J/KG) as well as a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels to result in the development of strong thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwest Arizona. Downdraft CAPE values were also in excess of 1500 J/KG, which led to favorable conditions for some of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of flash flooding on Peralta Road, east of Gold Canyon, which stranded motorists. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Image from social media showed the automatic doorframe to a Walmart, located in the intersection of Lindsay and Baseline Roads, pushed in. Winds were estimated between 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms on the 19th initiated across southeast Arizona and along the Mogollon Rim during the late morning/early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower elevations of south-central Arizona. A couple of upper level features, which included an area of low pressure over northern Sonora and a shortwave trough over the Great Basin, aided in ascent over the region in an environment characterized by high instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and anomalously high moisture (PWATs 1.8-2.2). Given the moisture in place, thunderstorms were able to become efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding across the area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed a wash flowing across a low water crossing south of Salome on Harquahala Road, making the roadway impassable. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (1.8-2.0) and an unstable environment (MUCAPE 2,000-4,000 J/kg) across south-central and southwest Arizona allowed for convective development to become strong to severe. Thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona before working toward the south into the lower deserts. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts upwards of 60-70 mph along with several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Litchfield Park measured a 61 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a favorable easterly flow, a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels moved across southwest Arizona into southeast California, resulting in diurnally induced thunderstorm activity across the area during the early afternoon hours. High instability (SBCAPE 2000+ J/KG) and very high moisture content (PWAT 2+ inches) resulted in a favorable environment for strong thunderstorms leading to flash flooding to materialize. Flash flooding was reported along several stretches of SR-95 in La Paz County. Radar estimates of 1-3 inches were observed along the area that reported the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported along SR-95 northbound near the Yuma and La Paz County Line. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and tree branches were downed along McDowell Rd near North Miller Road, including a snapped palm tree. A restaurant sign was also blown over. Timing estimated from radar. Winds were estimated around 70 mph based on radar velocities in this area. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 66 mph gust was measured at Scottsdale Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Queen Creek posted on social media saying to avoid the area of Barnes Parkway and 212th Street due to a tree blown down across the road. Time and wind speed estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex developed and tracked into south-central Arizona during the early morning hours on the 4th with widespread heavy rainfall activity across the Phoenix area, leading to flash flooding. The thunderstorm complex continued to track westward through southwest Arizona and into the Lower Colorado River Valley through the morning hours while at the same time weakening in the process.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning was reported to have struck a generator at Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport, causing a server to go down and delay dozens of Southwest Airlines' flights for several hours. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media pictures showed multiple large tree downed across East Mesa, including one tree falling onto a house. Winds were estimated at around 70 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.75-2.00 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000+ J/KG) combined with an approaching upper-level trough to result in an environment favorable for strong convective activity. Thunderstorms first developed across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains during the early to mid afternoon hours and then slowly drifted southwestward towards south-central Arizona. Activity affected portions of Gila County, where significant water flow was observed along the Russell Gulch area. Additional activity moved from the higher terrain down into western portions of the Phoenix metro during the early evening hours, where damaging winds and blowing dust were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two semis flipped on I-10 westbound in Buckeye, just west of SR-85, causing both lanes to be temporarily blocked. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Image showed RV trailer and boat flipped at Lake Pleasant. Timing estimated from radar. Wind gust was estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple 12 diameter tree trunks snapped along 107th Ave just south of Happy Valley Road in Surprise. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media images showed several large uprooted trees in the Verrado area of Buckeye. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE in excess of 1500 J/KG were more than sufficient to lead to the development of thunderstorm activity, first developing across southeast Arizona and then being steered by the southeasterly flow into south-central Arizona. Portions of the East Valley got particularly hit hard between Gilbert and Mesa, where there were several reports of wind damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree fell onto and totaled a parked SUV at The Hamptons Apartments. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shed destroyed and blown near intersection with Southern and Spur. Shed only went across the street. Winds were estimated at around 50 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms on the 19th initiated across southeast Arizona and along the Mogollon Rim during the late morning/early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower elevations of south-central Arizona. A couple of upper level features, which included an area of low pressure over northern Sonora and a shortwave trough over the Great Basin, aided in ascent over the region in an environment characterized by high instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and anomalously high moisture (PWATs 1.8-2.2). Given the moisture in place, thunderstorms were able to become efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding across the area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An image showed Ashbrook Wash running across a low water crossing along El Pueblo Boulevard north of Grande Boulevard, making the road impassable. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility was reported along E Twin Acres Drive in Queen Creek in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level high pressure was situated across southern Nevada, with the local area under an northeasterly flow regime. At upper-levels, a disturbance moving northward out northern Baja California resulted in a diffluent flow, favorable for vertical ascent. The moisture and instability parameters were conducive to thunderstorm activity with PWATs 1.5+ inches and MLCAPE values ranging between 1500-2500 J/KG. As it is typical in a northeasterly flow regime, thunderstorms first began to develop along the Mogollon Rim early afternoon and then descended into the lower elevations during the mid to late afternoon hours, affecting the Greater Phoenix area and then tracking southwestward, affecting southwestern Arizona through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arizona Department of Transportation reported a crash along I-10 near Milepost 69, that was suspected to be attributed to blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow with visibilities falling less than 1/4 mile. A few storm chasers were also nearby on I-10, one with a photo of vehicles stopped in near zero visibility, one with a video showing dust first crossing I-10 near Milepost 67 and quickly falling to near zero visibility, and another stopped along I-10 near Milepost 77 in near zero visibilities. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge along East Fork Cave Creek Wash near North 7th Avenue in Phoenix indicated water levels rising above alarm stage at 2232 MST, with the flow peaking 3.77 feet (1557 cfs) at 2259 MST, and not going below alarm stage until 2359 MST. This likely made North 7th Avenue impassable. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under an easterly wind flow, subtle disturbances in the upper-levels moved across the state, helping to instigate thunderstorm activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The strongest thunderstorm activity was concentrated from western Maricopa County through most of La Paz and Yuma Counties. Strong instability (SB CAPE of 2000+ J/KG) and high moisture content (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms, which led to instances of damaging wind gusts as well as flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 68 mph wind gust was measured at the Yuma International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms initially began across parts of southern Gila County, along with eastern Maricopa County and northeast Pinal County, producing locally heavy rainfall leading to isolated flash flooding. By the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 20th, new thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) aided in maintaining strong to severe thunderstorms into the Phoenix area as thunderstorms pushed toward the south and into the lower deserts during the evening. DCAPE around 1,000-1,200 J/kg promoted strong to severe downbursts with the strongest thunderstorms. Meanwhile, PWATs around 1.7-1.9 aided in thunderstorms being heavy rainfall producers, leading to several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted near 91st Avenue and Beardsley Road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to be a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southwest Arizona. A cluster of thunderstorms developed near Yuma during the mid-afternoon hours leading to flash flooding and damaging winds across the Fortuna Hills area. Thereafter, additional storm complexes developed during the late afternoon and evening hours across portions of Yuma and La Paz Counties, likely due to the combination of terrain lift as well as colliding outflows from the storms that developed earlier in the afternoon across portions of Imperial and Riverside Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Separate social media videos showed water from Fortuna Wash flowing over S Ave 14 1/2 E and E 48th Street, causing the streets to become impassable. Most of the rain, estimated around 1 to 2 inches fell over the headwaters of Fortuna Wash, 3 to 5 miles to the southeast of Fortuna Foothills. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms across south-central Arizona on the 14th. Daytime heating aided in thunderstorm development across the high terrain north of Phoenix before sending outflows traveling toward the south into the lower elevations. New thunderstorm development occurred along these outflows into the Valley where they produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, and isolated flash flooding. Additional thunderstorm development occurred across southwest Arizona where better moisture allowed thunderstorms to be efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down in the vicinity of 36th Drive as well as 24th Street between Avenues B and C. Images showed a large uprooted tree down on a camper while another large downed tree fell onto a car. Timing was estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport ASOS measured a wind gust of 86 mph. This wind gust speed was investigated further as it was considered suspect, however NWS meteorologists determined there was not enough evidence to disprove this measurement. Centerfield winds, a 30 meter wind sensor located just over half a mile north of the ASOS, measured a 76 mph wind gust around the same time as the ASOS. The City of Phoenix Aviation Department shared a report with images that mentioned/showed more than a dozen downed trees, damage to some fencing, cargo containers that were damaged after nearly being blown into a building, at least one downed light pole, and a baggage cart that was blown into and damaged a blast fence. There was also damage to the 24th Street Sky Train station with some broken glass windows at one of the entrances and a small section of outer side paneling that was peeled off. Sky Harbor Airport was in a Ground Stop from 2125MST to 2154MST. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snapmap video showed an Amazon Semi-truck blown over its side along SR-87 and Gilbert Road. Winds were estimated at 70 kts from radar. Timing also estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

This was near the intersection of North Mesa Drive and East McKellips Road in Mesa. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to be a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southwest Arizona. A cluster of thunderstorms developed near Yuma during the mid-afternoon hours leading to flash flooding and damaging winds across the Fortuna Hills area. Thereafter, additional storm complexes developed during the late afternoon and evening hours across portions of Yuma and La Paz Counties, likely due to the combination of terrain lift as well as colliding outflows from the storms that developed earlier in the afternoon across portions of Imperial and Riverside Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous images of microburst wind damage in the Fortuna Foothills area. Trees were uprooted, brick walls toppled, sheet metal carports destroyed, shingles of roofs were ripped off, downed power poles including a wooden pole that snapped and fell on a carport, a semi-truck trailer flipped on its side, broken window, a garage door blown in, and many other minor damages. The damage may have been caused by multiple microbursts. Timing estimated from radar. Winds were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated south of the International Border led to a westerly flow aloft, which caused dry air advection. However, there was still sufficient moisture (PWAT around 1.5 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000-2000 J/KG) as well as a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels to result in the development of strong thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwest Arizona. Downdraft CAPE values were also in excess of 1500 J/KG, which led to favorable conditions for some of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous downed trees with diameters ranging from 6-24 inches in the area of West Beardsley Parkway and North Desert Oasis Boulevard in Sun City Festival. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were knocked down with trunk diameters ranging from 6-12 inches along the area of Sun Valley Parkway and North Desert Oasis Boulevard in Sun City Festival. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with divergence aloft associated with an easterly wave near the Arizona/New Mexico border promoted thunderstorm initiation across southeast Arizona during the afternoon of the 30th. Outflow boundaries generated from these thunderstorms raced northwest toward south-central Arizona, resulting in a dust storm in Pinal County where multiple instances of dense blowing dust less than 1/4 mile were reported. New thunderstorm development occurred along these boundaries with some thunderstorms becoming severe. Numerous instances of damaging winds were reported across the Phoenix area and down into northwest Pinal County, which included multiple downed trees and power poles. A very moist environment, with PWATs in excess of 1.80 allowed for thunderstorms to become efficient heavy rainfall producers. Rainfall amounts upwards of around 1.50 within an hour were reported during the event. There were multiple reports of flash flooding across northern parts of the Phoenix area, leading to several water rescues and making some roads impassable. Flash flooding also led to water and mud entering some apartments in north Phoenix causing extensive water damage. Isolated flash flooding occurred across Pinal, Gila, and Yuma Counties. No injuries were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 60 mph were measured with a handheld anemometer. Multiple 10-inch diameter Palo Verde trees were reported to have been knocked over in the area. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms across south-central Arizona on the 14th. Daytime heating aided in thunderstorm development across the high terrain north of Phoenix before sending outflows traveling toward the south into the lower elevations. New thunderstorm development occurred along these outflows into the Valley where they produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, and isolated flash flooding. Additional thunderstorm development occurred across southwest Arizona where better moisture allowed thunderstorms to be efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust reduced visibility below 1/8 mile along I-10 near milepost 186. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture shows Russell Rd near Utility St impassable due to flash flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Phoenix-Mesa Gateway Airport measured a wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.75-2.00 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000+ J/KG) combined with an approaching upper-level trough to result in an environment favorable for strong convective activity. Thunderstorms first developed across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains during the early to mid afternoon hours and then slowly drifted southwestward towards south-central Arizona. Activity affected portions of Gila County, where significant water flow was observed along the Russell Gulch area. Additional activity moved from the higher terrain down into western portions of the Phoenix metro during the early evening hours, where damaging winds and blowing dust were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were down in the area between Happy Valley Rd and Deer Valley Rd and between 225th Ave and US-60 in Surprise. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

The environment was characterized by a very moist air mass (PWATs 2+ inches) and a highly unstable air mass (MLCAPE 1500+ J/KG). These favorable parameters resulted in the development of strong thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant wind damage was observed in the Saddles of Joy property, located just southwest of Yuma Airport, from a thunderstorm microburst. Based on the images, large trees were uprooted, there was significant fencing damage, and destroyed sheds. A GoFundMe for 10K was set up to help with the damage cost. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a favorable easterly flow, a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels moved across southwest Arizona into southeast California, resulting in diurnally induced thunderstorm activity across the area during the early afternoon hours. High instability (SBCAPE 2000+ J/KG) and very high moisture content (PWAT 2+ inches) resulted in a favorable environment for strong thunderstorms leading to flash flooding to materialize. Flash flooding was reported along several stretches of SR-95 in La Paz County. Radar estimates of 1-3 inches were observed along the area that reported the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along SR-95 Northbound near Cyprus Mine Road. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the southern Plains and low pressure across the Great Basin led to a southerly flow across the region, advecting deep moisture. PWATs rose to 2, which was near record for the period, and with the increased moisture instability values also rose with MLCAPE values in excess of 2,000 J/KG. The forcing from the aforementioned low pressure helped to enhance the thunderstorm activity as it was moving from south to north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed Arizona Department of Public Safety performed a swift water rescue by air around 2100MST. A family of 6 (2 parents and 4 kids) in an SUV were washed off of Val Vista Road near Warren Road at a low water crossing with Vekol Wash. Floodwaters were over the hood of the SUV after being pushed downstream. All 6 individuals were safely rescued from the roof of their vehicle and were unharmed. A Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge on Vekol Wash near I-8, located approximately 14 miles southwest, showed the flow going above alarm stage at 1340 MST, peaking at 7.80 feet (2465 cfs) at 1540 MST, and returning below alarm stage at 1754 MST. A news article mentioned this was the second swift water rescue performed in a matter of a few hours. Details on the other rescue were not found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Adequate moisture and instability in place across south-central Arizona on the 14th allowed for thunderstorm development to occur across the high terrain areas of Arizona during the afternoon. Going through the evening hours, multiple outflow boundaries descended into the Valley triggering additional thunderstorm development in the lower deserts. Some of these thunderstorm became strong, including one thunderstorm that developed over the Queen Creek area, producing strong winds. As a result of the strong winds, multiple trees and power poles were knocked down in Queen Creek. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a thunderstorm downburst resulted in downed trees and power lines with pictures showing several power poles blown over along Ocotillo Road near Sossaman Road in Queen Creek. A person traveling along Ocotillo Road at the time became trapped after power lines fell onto their car. The individual escaped the incident without any injury. Ocotillo Road between Sossaman Road and Power Road was closed as crews worked to repair power poles and power lines. Winds in the area are believed to have climbed upwards of 55 mph. Nearby, Phoenix-Mesa Gateway Airport recorded a 52 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level high pressure was situated across southern Nevada, with the local area under an northeasterly flow regime. At upper-levels, a disturbance moving northward out northern Baja California resulted in a diffluent flow, favorable for vertical ascent. The moisture and instability parameters were conducive to thunderstorm activity with PWATs 1.5+ inches and MLCAPE values ranging between 1500-2500 J/KG. As it is typical in a northeasterly flow regime, thunderstorms first began to develop along the Mogollon Rim early afternoon and then descended into the lower elevations during the mid to late afternoon hours, affecting the Greater Phoenix area and then tracking southwestward, affecting southwestern Arizona through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media picture showed a large tree uprooted alongside a home located on the intersection of West Cinnabar Avenue and North 33rd Avenue in Glendale. Timing estimated from radar. Winds were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Mogollon Rim to the Valley (Rim-to-Valley), with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada, a multicell storm cluster descended out of the White Mountains, through Gila County, and into the lower deserts of Maricopa and Pinal Counties in the early overnight hours of the 1st. Moisture levels were near average for the time of year, but steep mid level lapse rates over the high terrain (7-8 C/km) and modest shear (Sfc-6km around 20-25 kts) was sufficient to support the development of an organized multicell storm cluster. The environment was also supportive of strong downbursts, with DCAPE up around 1000-1500 J/kg. A single ordinary storm also developed in the Queen Creek/San Tan Valley area a couple hours before the multicell cluster moved into the lower deserts, and produced a damaging downburst. Strong winds leading to a dust storm was eventually generated by the multicell cluster once it moved into Maricopa and Pinal Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Arizona Department of Transportation traffic web cam at the intersection of State Route 347 and Cement Plant Road showed near zero visibility in blowing dust. No injuries or significant impacts to traffic were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance tracking through the southern half of Arizona along with moderate instability with MUCAPE values greater than 1000 J/kg and 20-30 kts of deep layer shear resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms first began to develop during the late afternoon hours across the higher terrain, in southern Gila County, and then descended towards the lower elevations, affecting the Phoenix metro, during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media images showed a large uprooted tree caused damage to a sidewalk. Winds were estimated at 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance tracking through the southern half of Arizona along with moderate instability with MUCAPE values greater than 1000 J/kg and 20-30 kts of deep layer shear resulted in a favorable environment for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms.
