EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed roof torn off of the Bales Hay Farm and Ranch Feed Store located on West Beloat Road in Buckeye. Winds were estimated at 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large Mcdonald's billboard sign fell on two cars parked at the Colonade Motel located on East Main Street between North 54th and North 56th Street in Mesa. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter measured a 62 mph wind gust with handheld anemometer from his home located near the intersection of Baseline and Rural Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.75-2.00 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000+ J/KG) combined with an approaching upper-level trough to result in an environment favorable for strong convective activity. Thunderstorms first developed across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains during the early to mid afternoon hours and then slowly drifted southwestward towards south-central Arizona. Activity affected portions of Gila County, where significant water flow was observed along the Russell Gulch area. Additional activity moved from the higher terrain down into western portions of the Phoenix metro during the early evening hours, where damaging winds and blowing dust were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Surprise Emergency Manager said there was significant flash flooding in the Asante Community, with evidence of water over the roads. The Emergency Manager also shared there was significant mud and runoff at a water treatment plant near 29 Palms RV Park. On social media there were several reports of at least minor street flooding around the area as well. Timing of impacts estimated from radar. No reports of damage or injuries from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images showed several snapped power poles in the area of Johnson Road and Yuma Road. Downed trees were also reported in the area. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with divergence aloft associated with an easterly wave near the Arizona/New Mexico border promoted thunderstorm initiation across southeast Arizona during the afternoon of the 30th. Outflow boundaries generated from these thunderstorms raced northwest toward south-central Arizona, resulting in a dust storm in Pinal County where multiple instances of dense blowing dust less than 1/4 mile were reported. New thunderstorm development occurred along these boundaries with some thunderstorms becoming severe. Numerous instances of damaging winds were reported across the Phoenix area and down into northwest Pinal County, which included multiple downed trees and power poles. A very moist environment, with PWATs in excess of 1.80 allowed for thunderstorms to become efficient heavy rainfall producers. Rainfall amounts upwards of around 1.50 within an hour were reported during the event. There were multiple reports of flash flooding across northern parts of the Phoenix area, leading to several water rescues and making some roads impassable. Flash flooding also led to water and mud entering some apartments in north Phoenix causing extensive water damage. Isolated flash flooding occurred across Pinal, Gila, and Yuma Counties. No injuries were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple toppled or snapped wooden power poles were reported along Rodeo Road west of SR 387. Additional downed power poles were reported along McCartney Road nearby. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported along the entrance ramp at Princess Drive onto the Loop 101 in Scottsdale. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms initially began across parts of southern Gila County, along with eastern Maricopa County and northeast Pinal County, producing locally heavy rainfall leading to isolated flash flooding. By the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 20th, new thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) aided in maintaining strong to severe thunderstorms into the Phoenix area as thunderstorms pushed toward the south and into the lower deserts during the evening. DCAPE around 1,000-1,200 J/kg promoted strong to severe downbursts with the strongest thunderstorms. Meanwhile, PWATs around 1.7-1.9 aided in thunderstorms being heavy rainfall producers, leading to several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A FCDMC streamflow gage along Sols Wash exceeded alarm stage, making a low water crossing along Vulture Mine Road impassable. At 0424MST, the gage peaked at a depth of 3.65 feet and a flow of 3,822 CFS. A social media video showed Sols Wash flowing across Vulture Mine Road and Wickenburg dispatch reported the road was closed as a result of the flooding. No damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flow in Centennial Wash caused a low water crossing along Centennial Park Road to become impassable. An RV attempted to cross the flooded roadway but was swept off the road. Images also showed damage to the roadway from the flooding. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to be a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southwest Arizona. A cluster of thunderstorms developed near Yuma during the mid-afternoon hours leading to flash flooding and damaging winds across the Fortuna Hills area. Thereafter, additional storm complexes developed during the late afternoon and evening hours across portions of Yuma and La Paz Counties, likely due to the combination of terrain lift as well as colliding outflows from the storms that developed earlier in the afternoon across portions of Imperial and Riverside Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on SR-72, west of McVay Road. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the southern Plains and low pressure across the Great Basin led to a southerly flow across the region, advecting deep moisture. PWATs rose to 2, which was near record for the period, and with the increased moisture instability values also rose with MLCAPE values in excess of 2,000 J/KG. The forcing from the aforementioned low pressure helped to enhance the thunderstorm activity as it was moving from south to north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseline Rd in the Palo Verde region was closed between Johnson Rd and 331st Ave due to flooding. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company reported 4 power poles were downed in a couple locations north of Stanfield. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 15 downed and/or snapped power poles along North 35th Avenue, near West Bell Road in Phoenix. Timing estimated from radar. Wind gust was estimated at 60-70 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were reported down near Castle Hot Springs Road north of SR 74. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under an easterly wind flow, subtle disturbances in the upper-levels moved across the state, helping to instigate thunderstorm activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The strongest thunderstorm activity was concentrated from western Maricopa County through most of La Paz and Yuma Counties. Strong instability (SB CAPE of 2000+ J/KG) and high moisture content (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms, which led to instances of damaging wind gusts as well as flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine power poles were knocked down/damaged near 801 E 32nd Street in Yuma. Timing estimated from radar. Winds were estimated between 65-70 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images showed multiple snapped and uprooted trees in the Sun City Festival area. Some trees knocked down road signs and also fell onto walls and a pergola. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE in excess of 1500 J/KG were more than sufficient to lead to the development of thunderstorm activity, first developing across southeast Arizona and then being steered by the southeasterly flow into south-central Arizona. Portions of the East Valley got particularly hit hard between Gilbert and Mesa, where there were several reports of wind damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Gilbert Police of numerous downed trees and debris on roads. Winds were estimated at 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media images showed large trees down in the intersection of Lindsay with Baseline Roads. One of the trees fell onto and destroyed a car. There was also an image that showed a street sign toppled in the intersection of Lindsay with Melody Drive. Winds were estimated between 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of near record moisture content (PWATs 1.8-2.0 inches), moderate to strong instability (SBCAPE  2000 J/KG), very slow steering flow and an upper-level disturbance resulted in a very conducive environment for the generation of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona, resulting in flash flooding. Activity first developed across the foothills and higher terrain of Eastern Maricopa and Gila Counties during the late morning-early afternoon hours. Another area of thunderstorms developed over northwestern Arizona during the mid-late afternoon hours and move southward towards La Paz County into the evening hours. One of the areas severely hit by flash flooding was Apache Junction, AZ. Weekes Wash, which runs through the town, went of out of its banks, causing widespread flooding impacts across the city. Flooding also took place from drainage that occurred off the Goldfield and Superstitions Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed Bloody Tanks Wash in major elevated stage as it was flowing down the city of Miami. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms on the 19th initiated across southeast Arizona and along the Mogollon Rim during the late morning/early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower elevations of south-central Arizona. A couple of upper level features, which included an area of low pressure over northern Sonora and a shortwave trough over the Great Basin, aided in ascent over the region in an environment characterized by high instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and anomalously high moisture (PWATs 1.8-2.2). Given the moisture in place, thunderstorms were able to become efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding across the area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A FCDMC streamflow gage along Delaney Wash exceeded alarm stage, impacting a downstream low water crossing along 411th Avenue. At 2139MST, the gage peaked at a depth of 2.94 feet and a flow of 426 CFS. No damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long travelling outflow boundary produced estimated gusts to around 35-40 mph, uprooting a large palo verde tree. The tree landed on a pickup truck, resulting in damage. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and high DCAPE values around 1,500 J/kg allowed for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop and produce isolated damaging winds in Yuma County. Several downed power poles were reported in Dome Valley. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An image showed approximately five downed power poles in Dome Valley near County 8th Street and Avenue 20 E. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts upwards of 60-70 mph along with several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in San Tan Valley measured a 64 mph gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 77 mph wind gust was measured at the Buckeye Municipal Airport. Triple G Dairy Farm, just to the south of the airport, experienced a lot of damage, including shattered windows from flying debris or hail, a few downed power poles, uprooted trees, metal roof partially torn off one building, and at least minor damage to nearby homes owned by dairy workers. A month after the storm, NWS Phoenix was contacted by a claims specialist looking into a hangar door that was blown off at the airport. Although unconfirmed, this storm, with the 77 mph gust, is the most probable cause of the hangar door damage. Estimated damage costs for the hangar door are applied to this event. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images showed a destroyed carport with the metal roofing blown into nearby power lines at a mobile home community. Other images showed several large power poles tilted over along 91st Avenue north of Peoria Avenue. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed nickel to quarter-sized hail fell in the area between West Glendale Avenue and North 35th Avenue. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small trees were knocked over near Reems Road and Cactus Road. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE in excess of 1500 J/KG were more than sufficient to lead to the development of thunderstorm activity, first developing across southeast Arizona and then being steered by the southeasterly flow into south-central Arizona. Portions of the East Valley got particularly hit hard between Gilbert and Mesa, where there were several reports of wind damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Semi-truck flipped over due to strong winds on I-8 eastbound near MP 157. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms on the 19th initiated across southeast Arizona and along the Mogollon Rim during the late morning/early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower elevations of south-central Arizona. A couple of upper level features, which included an area of low pressure over northern Sonora and a shortwave trough over the Great Basin, aided in ascent over the region in an environment characterized by high instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and anomalously high moisture (PWATs 1.8-2.2). Given the moisture in place, thunderstorms were able to become efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding across the area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A FCDMC streamflow gage along Cave Creek exceeded alarm stage, impacting downstream low water crossings such as Canyon Creek Road. At 1809MST, the gage peaked at a depth of 6.89 feet and a flow of 3,670 CFS. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (1.8-2.0) and an unstable environment (MUCAPE 2,000-4,000 J/kg) across south-central and southwest Arizona allowed for convective development to become strong to severe. Thunderstorms initiated over the high terrain of northern Arizona before working toward the south into the lower deserts. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts upwards of 60-70 mph along with several reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in San Tan Valley measured a 69 mph gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood Control District of Maricopa County stream gage along Jackrabbit Wash exceeded alarm stage at 2236 MST, making the Vulture Mine Road low water crossing impassable and mostly likely Wickenburg Road, located 5 miles downstream. Flow peaked at 3.35 feet and 1034 CFS at 2305 MST and dropped below alarm stage at 2340 MST. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast video showed a lady stuck at a skate park located along the Indian Bend Wash near McDowell Road in Scottsdale. Rescuers unable to get to her for a couple of hours at least due to the flood waters of several feet along the wash. An aerial rescue was performed around 2215 MST. Just 2 miles upstream along the wash near Indian School Road, a Flood County District of Maricopa County gauge reported water levels going above alarm stage after 1830 MST, rising to a record stage of 5.76 feet (3929 cfs) at around 1907 MST. The water level did not go below flood stage until several hours after, at 230 MST on the 13th. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video images from social media showed raging flood waters occurring near Salome Highway just east of US-60. Timing estimated from radar. In addition, the Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge located along Centennial Wash in Wenden showed water levels rising above alarm stage at around 1950 MST, peaking at 3.37 feet (1323 cfs) at around 2034 MST and not going below alarm stage until around 2330 MST. The impacted areas from Centennial Wash include Salome Road, which is located about 4.8 miles downstream of the gauge location in Wenden. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple downed trees reported in the Villages of Queen Creek Subdivision. Wind speeds estimated around 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report on social media of lost roof tiles from home and tree down near the area of South Meridian Road and East Pima Road. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad southeast flow associated with the circulation of Tropical Storm Kay, which was located just west of the Baja California Peninsula, set up across Arizona. This helped in advecting moisture into the area with PWAT's in excess of 1.5. Instability was also present with MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG. In addition, there was upper-level diffluence over south-central Arizona, which helped enhance the shower and thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads that intersect Waterman Wash in the Rainbow Valley/Buckeye area were impassable/closed due to flooding. This included Riggs Road, Tuthill Road between between Pecos Road and Fairview Street, and Narramore Road between Dean Road and Airport Road. A Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge of Waterman Wash at Rainbow Valley Road reported a peak flow of 6.12 feet (1151 cfs), which was 2.82 feet above alarm stage. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than 1/4 mile reported along East Ray Road in the Ahwatukee Foothills in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR-238 was closed in its entirety between Gila Bend and SR-347 due to significant flooding. The Maricopa Fire and Medical Crew as well as the Goodyear Fire Department in a collective effort had to rescue approximately 32 people in stranded vehicles. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms across south-central Arizona on the 14th. Daytime heating aided in thunderstorm development across the high terrain north of Phoenix before sending outflows traveling toward the south into the lower elevations. New thunderstorm development occurred along these outflows into the Valley where they produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, and isolated flash flooding. Additional thunderstorm development occurred across southwest Arizona where better moisture allowed thunderstorms to be efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding. No injuries were reported from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust reduced visibility below 1/4 mile along I-10 near milepost 181. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a favorable easterly flow, a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels moved across southwest Arizona into southeast California, resulting in diurnally induced thunderstorm activity across the area during the early afternoon hours. High instability (SBCAPE 2000+ J/KG) and very high moisture content (PWAT 2+ inches) resulted in a favorable environment for strong thunderstorms leading to flash flooding to materialize. Flash flooding was reported along several stretches of SR-95 in La Paz County. Radar estimates of 1-3 inches were observed along the area that reported the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported along SR-95 Northbound near Blevens Road. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under an easterly wind flow, subtle disturbances in the upper-levels moved across the state, helping to instigate thunderstorm activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The strongest thunderstorm activity was concentrated from western Maricopa County through most of La Paz and Yuma Counties. Strong instability (SB CAPE of 2000+ J/KG) and high moisture content (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms, which led to instances of damaging wind gusts as well as flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR-238 was completely closed between Gila Bend and SR-347 due to flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated south of the International Border led to a westerly flow aloft, which caused dry air advection. However, there was still sufficient moisture (PWAT around 1.5 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000-2000 J/KG) as well as a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels to result in the development of strong thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwest Arizona. Downdraft CAPE values were also in excess of 1500 J/KG, which led to favorable conditions for some of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Flood Control District of Maricopa County Stream Gauge of Jackrabbit Wash at Vulture Mine Road reported water levels rising rapidly above alarm stage at around 1713 MST, with a peak flow of 4.88 feet (3651 cfs) at 1803 MST, and going back down below alarm stage at around 1926 MST. This amount of water flow likely flooded Aguila Road upstream of the gauge and Wickenburg Road, where a water rescue was performed, located 5 miles downstream of the gauge. No injuries were reported. Based on the report from the Phoenix Dispatch, water got halfway up on an unoccupied vehicle stuck along the flood waters from Jackrabbit Wash located along Wickenburg Road.

Thunderstorms first began to develop during the late afternoon hours across the higher terrain, in southern Gila County, and then descended towards the lower elevations, affecting the Phoenix metro, during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from spotter of solar panels peeled off roofs and large trees 1-2 feet downed near the intersection of West Southern Avenue and South Miller Road in Buckeye. Winds were estimated at 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesa Falcon Field Airport AWOS Station measured a 64 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported along Loop-101 near East Cactus Road in Scottsdale. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge along Jackrabbit Wash just downstream of North Vulture Mine Road indicated water levels rising above the alarm stage of 2.9 feet at 1800 MST. The flow peaked at 5.70 feet (6000 cfs) at around 1910 MST.  Impacted area included Wickenburg Road, located about 5 miles downstream of the gauge. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed Indian Bend Wash running multiple feet deep, resulting in a car nearly submerged by the running flood waters along McCormick Parkway in Scottsdale. The person inside the vehicle had to be rescued by the Fire Department. A Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge along the Indian Bend Wash at Shea Boulevard, located about 3 miles upstream of the incident showed peak flow of 2.92 feet (1875 cfs), which was above alarm stage, at 1640 MST. Additional flow of just over 1000 cfs was likely added to the wash from the Berneil Wash, located about 1 mile upstream of the incident. The gauge along the Indian Bend Wash at Shea Boulevard did not go below alarm stage until just after 2015 MST. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the 20th, thunderstorms once again initiated across the northern Arizona high terrain during the afternoon of the 21st, though more focused along the Mogollon Rim compared to the day prior. Increased steering flow allowed for thunderstorms to move off of the Rim and toward the lower elevations. High instability (MUCAPE 2,000-2,500 J/kg) helped thunderstorms maintain intensity as they travelled toward the lower deserts. Given the greater steering flow, faster thunderstorm motion helped abate flash flood impacts. Greater shear across northwest Arizona allowed an organized complex of thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours before progressing through southwest Arizona and the Lower Colorado River Valley area, producing severe wind gusts and dense blowing dust into the early morning hours on the 22nd. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yuma Proving Ground Station 6 mesonet recorded a 64 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable monsoonal conditions promoted the development of severe thunderstorms across the region on the 18th. Abundant moisture (PWATs of 1.7-2.0) and instability (MUCAPE 1,000-2,000 J/kg) was in place across south-central and southwest Arizona, while increased easterly steering flow from an upper level low over northern Mexico aided in thunderstorms propagating off the high terrain. DCAPE were around 1,000-1,200 J/kg which helped in allowing strong to severe thunderstorms to produce damaging winds from downbursts. Thunderstorms initially developed along the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower deserts of south-central and southwest Arizona through the evening. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were reported down in Hyder near Palomas Road. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms on the 19th initiated across southeast Arizona and along the Mogollon Rim during the late morning/early afternoon hours before pushing into the lower elevations of south-central Arizona. A couple of upper level features, which included an area of low pressure over northern Sonora and a shortwave trough over the Great Basin, aided in ascent over the region in an environment characterized by high instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and anomalously high moisture (PWATs 1.8-2.2). Given the moisture in place, thunderstorms were able to become efficient rainfall producers, leading to multiple reports of flash flooding across the area. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MCDOT reported that Centennial Road between 517th Avenue and 555th Avenue was closed due to flooding in Centennial Wash. Timing estimated from radar. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant mid-level circulation of what was Tropical Cyclone Kay was located over southern California. This led to southerly flow across Arizona, which helped increased moisture and instability with moisture values peaking at around 1.5 and MLCAPE values peaking at around 1000 J/KG. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona, near the Tucson area, during the late afternoon hours of the 11th and then progressed northward towards the Phoenix metro during the evening hours, resulting in widespread severe downburst winds and blowing dust as well as isolated flash flooding. The Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reported a peak wind gust of 86 mph at 2053 MST. Additional storms then developed across western Maricopa County, extending into La Paz County, during the early morning hours of the 12th with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding become the biggest threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company reported 2 downed power poles in 2 locations east of Maricopa. Winds were estimated around 60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a classical monsoonal regime, with deep-layered east to southeast flow, high instability (MLCAPE in excess of 1000-1500 J/KG) along with abundant moisture (PWAT 1.8+ inches) resulted in an environment favorable for thunderstorms. An upper-level disturbance moving across Arizona, aided in increasing the areal coverage as well as enhancing the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of thunderstorm damage across Quartzsite. The damages reported included several trailers/RVs flipped, signs blown around, trees blown over, heavy grills thrown 50 feet, storage sheds destroyed, leaning power poles.  Timing estimated from radar. A wind gust of 65 mph was measured from a amateur radio user. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of near record moisture content (PWATs 1.8-2.0 inches), moderate to strong instability (SBCAPE  2000 J/KG), very slow steering flow and an upper-level disturbance resulted in a very conducive environment for the generation of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona, resulting in flash flooding. Activity first developed across the foothills and higher terrain of Eastern Maricopa and Gila Counties during the late morning-early afternoon hours. Another area of thunderstorms developed over northwestern Arizona during the mid-late afternoon hours and move southward towards La Paz County into the evening hours. One of the areas severely hit by flash flooding was Apache Junction, AZ. Weekes Wash, which runs through the town, went of out of its banks, causing widespread flooding impacts across the city. Flooding also took place from drainage that occurred off the Goldfield and Superstitions Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell within 1-2 hours across Apache Junction and the adjacent higher terrain north and east of the city, towards the Goldfield and Superstitions mountains. Water from those mountains drained down towards the area washes, including Weekes Wash, which runs through the city. Numerous roads were flooded and damaged along with multiple water rescues taking place of residents in homes that either were surrounded with water or had water inside them. One of the more dramatic water rescues occurred when a woman had to be rescued from her vehicle that was swept away by the flood waters of Weekes Wash and traveled half a mile downstream before encountering a box culvert. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated south of the International Border led to a westerly flow aloft, which caused dry air advection. However, there was still sufficient moisture (PWAT around 1.5 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000-2000 J/KG) as well as a subtle disturbance in the upper-levels to result in the development of strong thunderstorms across portions of south-central and southwest Arizona. Downdraft CAPE values were also in excess of 1500 J/KG, which led to favorable conditions for some of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous downed trees (10+) along the area of Higley and Germann Roads in Gilbert. Timing estimated from radar. Winds were estimated at around 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level flow of 25-30 kts and divergence aloft combined with sufficient MLCAPE of  greater than 1000 J/kg to result in an environment favorable for the development of organized severe thunderstorms. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southeast Pinal County during the late afternoon hours and moved northwestward through the Phoenix Metro after dark while becoming severe in the process. One of the more significant damage areas was along SR-87 in Salt River Reservation Community, where a mobile home and trailer park were destroyed and large transmission lines were downed. Power was knocked out for some for more than 2 days. A large dust storm also impacted parts of Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was measured at Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by an area of high pressure centered across the four corners region with a southeasterly steering flow encompassing all of Arizona. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE in excess of 1500 J/KG were more than sufficient to lead to the development of thunderstorm activity, first developing across southeast Arizona and then being steered by the southeasterly flow into south-central Arizona. Portions of the East Valley got particularly hit hard between Gilbert and Mesa, where there were several reports of wind damage. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos from an employee of the Salt River Project Utility Company showed several large trees down as well as a street sign and a light post toppled. Winds were estimated between 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

Thunderstorms first began to develop during the late afternoon hours across the higher terrain, in southern Gila County, and then descended towards the lower elevations, affecting the Phoenix metro, during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was measured at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the southern Plains and low pressure across the Great Basin led to a southerly flow across the region, advecting deep moisture. PWATs rose to 2, which was near record for the period, and with the increased moisture instability values also rose with MLCAPE values in excess of 2,000 J/KG. The forcing from the aforementioned low pressure helped to enhance the thunderstorm activity as it was moving from south to north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flood Control District of Maricopa County Gauge along Centennial Wash near the Railroad area reached action stage at 954 MST, with a peak flow of 10.12 feet (1341 cfs) at 1112 MST. Flash Flood impacts downstream where Centennial Wash crosses old U.S. 80 occurred 1.5 hours after action stage was reached, which is 6 miles downstream of the gauge. The flow returned below alarm stage at 1507 MST. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a classic setup for storm motion from the Northern Arizona high terrain to Southern Arizona lower deserts, with the monsoon 500mb high positioned over Nevada and Utah, a few multicell storm clusters developed and progressed west-southwest through the lower deserts in the afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Increased east-northeast steering flow resulted in relatively high shear values for the monsoon (Sfc-6km shear around 25-30 kts) and very steep mid-level lapse rate (8-8.5 C/km) supported the organized multicellular storm mode with robust updrafts. Inversely, very high DCAPE values, up to 1500-1800 J/kg, supported very strong downbursts. Moisture levels were also above normal which, in combination with the strong updrafts, supported high rainfall rates (1.5-2 inches per hour average). There were a few multicell clusters that moved through Southern Arizona. One through the southeast parts of the greater Phoenix area, another that developed in Yavapai County before descending southwest along US-60 in Maricopa and La Paz Counties, and a third that developed off the Kofa Wilderness terrain south of Quartzsite before being caught by the cluster descending along US-60. Each cluster produced damaging winds, dense blowing dust, small to severe hail, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 1/4 mile reported along the intersection of 1-10 with SR-387 in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

Timing estimated based off when the water level first reached 1 feet deep. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of moisture (PWAT 1.5+) and moderate to high instability (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG) led to the development of terrain and diurnally induced thunderstorm activity. Thunderstorms first began to develop across portions of southwestern Arizona during the early afternoon hours and thereafter, additional storm development occurred across the higher terrain east of Phoenix. Outflow from storms, particularly across the higher terrain east of Phoenix led to the development of more storms across portions of the Phoenix metro, particularly in the area between Chandler and Queen Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yuma Proving Ground Station measured a peak gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture (PWATs 1.75-2.00 inches) and instability (MLCAPE 1000+ J/KG) combined with an approaching upper-level trough to result in an environment favorable for strong convective activity. Thunderstorms first developed across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains during the early to mid afternoon hours and then slowly drifted southwestward towards south-central Arizona. Activity affected portions of Gila County, where significant water flow was observed along the Russell Gulch area. Additional activity moved from the higher terrain down into western portions of the Phoenix metro during the early evening hours, where damaging winds and blowing dust were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast showed footage of significant damage in the Buckeye area, including roofs partially torn off and a destroyed RV. No other specific details were received. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the upper-levels on the northeastern periphery of the circulation of Tropical Storm Javier, which was moving westward across the eastern Pacific, was enough to generate a couple of strong thunderstorms during the late afternoon hours across extreme southwest Arizona, around the vicinity of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 300 feet was reported near the area of SR-195 and I-8 in Yuma in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge situated over the Desert Southwest led to well above normal temperatures along with excessive heat conditions at times. Temperatures during this time at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked on the 16th at 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 64 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest persisted into the latter part of July, leading to temperatures in excess of 110 degrees in Phoenix most days during this time. Temperatures during this time period peaked on the 22nd with Phoenix Sky Harbor recording a high of 114 degrees. Overnight temperatures provided little relief from the heat as morning lows remained in the 90s for several days. On the 22nd, the morning low temperature only cooled down to 95 degrees in Phoenix. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 81 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 7 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona June 16, with high temperatures near record levels. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures occurred across western Pima and south central Pinal counties and caused the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Ajo and Picacho topped out at 110 degrees and Organ Pipe National Monument reached 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A breach in a berm along Rucker Creek near the northeast edge of Elfrida caused flash flooding in part of town on August 23. Additional rainfall in town and upstream of the breach exacerbated the flooding later that day. Heavy rain well upstream traveled downstream through the breach and flooded more of the town on August 24 and 25. Several homes sustained significant flood damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 at the headwaters of Rucker Creek traveled downstream and through and existing breach in a berm combined with rainfall in the immediate area, resulted in an exacerbation of flooding in Elfrida. Eleven sustained major damage and 3 were determined to be a total loss. A couple of businesses also sustained flood damage and floodwaters also affected septic tanks and wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries originating from distant thunderstorms converged on the valley floor during the evening hours. This combined with a pocket of upper-level divergence as analyzed by the SPC mesoanalysis resulted in the development of a strong thunderstorm over Chandler. Other weaker thunderstorms developed across the valley as well from the resulting outflow boundaries. A strong thunderstorm that developed near Morristown caused downed powerlines, resulting in the closure of US-60. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arizona Department of Transportation reported US-60 was closed in both directions in Morristown due to downed power lines. Winds were estimated at 50 mph. Timing of the damage was estimated based on radar. No injuries were reported.

There was also a popular social media photo of a possible landspout tornado in the Buckeye area. No damages or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 1/4 mile in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow on I-8 near Wellton. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category continued across eastern Santa Cruz and southern Cochise counties including the Huachuca and Mule Mountains through June. Two wildfires ignited in dry fuels in the middle of the month. The Raspberry fire in the Chiricahua Mountains and the Tonto Canyon fire in western Santa Cruz County were both extinguished later in the month, in part due to two to three inches of rainfall. The late June rain was enough to reduce both areas, along with the Dragoon Mountains, to the severe drought (D2) category toward the end of June. Soil moisture readings improved to just above the 30th percentile across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 7 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of Pima, Santa Cruz, Graham, Greenlee and southeast Pinal counties June 9-12, with high temperatures equaling or exceeding established records in several locations. Hot temperatures 4 to 7 degrees above normal were experienced in Cochise County. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for a total of 11 individuals.  Six individuals found in the desert, two on the Tohono O'odham Nation, one in Santa Cruz County, two in southeast Pima County and one in Tucson Mountain Park. Four victims were found in the Tucson (2 indoors and 2 outdoors), and another individual perished in his home in Bowie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures of 102 to 109 degrees occurred and caused the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Nogales airport set a record with 105 degrees on the 10th and Sasabe reached 109 degrees on the 11th and 12th. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert near Ruby on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built in across the Southwest heading into the middle part of June, bringing excessive heat conditions to the region. High temperatures in Phoenix officially approached or exceeded 110 degrees between June 8-12th, which is well above the seasonal normal. The hottest temperature during this timeframe occurred on the 11th when the high temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor peaked at 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 36 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved southwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening.  In the Tucson Metro area, damaging winds downed dozens of power poles and heavy rain resulted in flash flooding. Flash flooding also occurred in the Cochise County community of St. David.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1.5 in 1 hour caused flash flooding in southern Tucson. Swift water rescues from vehicles occurred near Bilby Rd. and Nogales Highway and Valencia Rd. and Park Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low located over the Great Basin produced cooler than normal 500 mb temperature of between -9 and -10C and also some upper-level diffluence with a 50-60 kt jet at upper-levels. Moisture levels were also above normal with precipitable water values between 1.0-1.3 inches. The combination of all of these factors resulted in sufficient instability with MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/KG while DCAPE values were approaching 1500 J/KG. Thunderstorms began to develop during the early to mid-afternoon hours across most of southern and central Arizona, with strong to locally damaging winds as well as dense blowing dust being the main hazards along with locally heavy rainfall. One of the damaged areas came just northwest of Stanfield, where there were multiple downed power poles as well as a rolled over RV. There were also visible photos from social media as well as from seasoned storm chasers of a couple of landspouts, one near damaged area just northwest of Stanfield and another over Buckeye. With respect to the landspout that developed just northwest of Stanfield, there was no confirmation that it was responsible for the damage observed, which most likely was due to downburst wind. No injuries were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility less than 1/4 mile in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow three miles northwest of Eloy on I-10. No accidents or injuries were reported.

These storms sent two outflow boundaries from opposing directions, which then collided across the Phoenix metro, sparking the development of additional storms over the Phoenix area. Another cluster of storms then developed across portions of southwest Arizona during the late afternoon into the early evening hours.  No damages or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 100 feet or less in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow around Milepost 81 of I-8 near Sentinel. No accidents or injuries were reported.

There was also a popular social media photo of a possible landspout tornado in the Buckeye area. No damages or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of near 1000 feet in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow reported near Tartron. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona. Thunderstorm winds produced a wall of dust and downed power lines northwest of Tucson while heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Baboquivari Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power lines near Santa Rosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona from mid morning through early evening. A couple of storms downed trees and produced flash flooding in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees near Skyline Dr. and Campbell Rd. in the Catalina Foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Kay were centered off the California coast but helped generate enough shear to produce severe thunderstorms that moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the late afternoon and evening. Thunderstorms produced large hail, heavy rain and flash flooding in Green Valley and isolated wind damage in Saddlebrooke and the northern part of the Tohono O'odham Nation.

Thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. In Tucson, power poles and trees were downed and some roads were flooded. Flash flooding also occurred in Nogales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-2' in 1 hour in Nogales, Sonora caused a rapid rise in Nogales Wash. The Doe St. bridge was overtopped and nearby roadways were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved north across southeast Arizona. Thunderstorm winds produced a wall of dust along I-10 in Pinal County.  Other storms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the Baboquivari Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 2 inches of rain in less than 2 hours over the Contreras burn scar caused a 1-2 ft debris flow across State Highway 386 to Kitt Peak.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds produced a wall of dust that reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile across I-10 northwest of Picacho.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category continued across eastern Santa Cruz and southern Cochise counties including the Huachuca and Mule Mountains through June. Two wildfires ignited in dry fuels in the middle of the month. The Raspberry fire in the Chiricahua Mountains and the Tonto Canyon fire in western Santa Cruz County were both extinguished later in the month, in part due to two to three inches of rainfall. The late June rain was enough to reduce both areas, along with the Dragoon Mountains, to the severe drought (D2) category toward the end of June. Soil moisture readings improved to just above the 30th percentile across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category continued across eastern Santa Cruz and southern Cochise counties including the Huachuca and Mule Mountains through June. Two wildfires ignited in dry fuels in the middle of the month. The Raspberry fire in the Chiricahua Mountains and the Tonto Canyon fire in western Santa Cruz County were both extinguished later in the month, in part due to two to three inches of rainfall. The late June rain was enough to reduce both areas, along with the Dragoon Mountains, to the severe drought (D2) category toward the end of June. Soil moisture readings improved to just above the 30th percentile across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low located over the Great Basin produced cooler than normal 500 mb temperature of between -9 and -10C and also some upper-level diffluence with a 50-60 kt jet at upper-levels. Moisture levels were also above normal with precipitable water values between 1.0-1.3 inches. The combination of all of these factors resulted in sufficient instability with MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/KG while DCAPE values were approaching 1500 J/KG. Thunderstorms began to develop during the early to mid-afternoon hours across most of southern and central Arizona, with strong to locally damaging winds as well as dense blowing dust being the main hazards along with locally heavy rainfall. One of the damaged areas came just northwest of Stanfield, where there were multiple downed power poles as well as a rolled over RV. There were also visible photos from social media as well as from seasoned storm chasers of a couple of landspouts, one near damaged area just northwest of Stanfield and another over Buckeye. With respect to the landspout that developed just northwest of Stanfield, there was no confirmation that it was responsible for the damage observed, which most likely was due to downburst wind. No injuries were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of 1/4 mile reported in Goodyear in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow along W Yuma Road between South Sarival Avenue and South Estrella Parkway. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure built in across the western United States for the end of May, allowing temperatures to climb several degrees above normal in Phoenix. At Phoenix Sky Harbor, temperatures peaked on the 27th at 106 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, this heat led to four heat-associated fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure was centered over the Central Plains during the middle part of the month with the Desert Southwest feeling its influence. This brought excessive heat conditions to the region with temperatures in Phoenix peaking at 113�� on the 16th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 9 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three fatalities (1 direct and 2 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northeast across southeast Arizona from late morning into early evening. One storm produced damaging winds in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power lines on Sanders Rd. causing its closure and the lost of power for more than 2000 customers. Winds also downed several Palo Verde trees near  Moore Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 5 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced across the western deserts of southeast Arizona on July 10 and 11. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in outdoors in Ajo on the 10th and for four individuals found the desert on the 11th: two on the Tohono O'odham Nation; one in east central Pima County; and one in Santa Cruz County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures around 10 degrees above normal occurred on July 11, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. The Tucson International Airport topped out at 110 degrees. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert south of Three Points.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries originating from distant thunderstorms converged on the valley floor during the evening hours. This combined with a pocket of upper-level divergence as analyzed by the SPC mesoanalysis resulted in the development of a strong thunderstorm over Chandler. Other weaker thunderstorms developed across the valley as well from the resulting outflow boundaries. A strong thunderstorm that developed near Morristown caused downed powerlines, resulting in the closure of US-60. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported along S Alma School Road between Sun Lakes and Chandler. Water was 2.5-3 inches deep. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved north across parts of southeast Arizona, mainly during the afternoon. One storm became severe and uprooted trees in Nogales and another storm produced flash flooding west of the Chiricahua Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 3-5 in 2 hours in the Chiricahua Mountains caused high flows on Turkey Creek. Fort Bowie Rd. was washed out well downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved southwest off the Mogollon Rim then turned west and became severe across Cochise and Santa Cruz counties during the late afternoon and evening. A microburst caused extensive damage to homes north of Douglas and severe wind gusts occurred farther west from Sierra Vista to Nogales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed 42 power poles north of Douglas. The downed poles caused the closure of Highway 191 for 24 hours. More than 40 residences sustained at least minor roof or cosmetic damage. This included 5 mobile homes that either sustained major damage or were completely destroyed and 3 other residences that had substantial damage. Most of these were east of Leslie Canyon Dr. near Madrone Dr. where a microburst occurred. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening, scattered thunderstorms moved southwest off the Mogollon Rim into Cochise County where one storm produced hail that damaged drops near Pearce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail combined with wind gusts to 50 mph damaged sunflower crops north of Pearce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona from mid morning through early evening. A couple of storms downed trees and produced flash flooding in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree near Swan Rd. north of River Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Localized flash flooding occurred in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of more than 1 in 1 hour caused flash flooding and closures of residential streets south of Orange Grove Road between La Cholla and La Canada. Additionally, a man was rescued after becoming trapped under a bridge by floodwaters near Blacklidge and Walnut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 7 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of Pima, Santa Cruz, Graham, Greenlee and southeast Pinal counties June 9-12, with high temperatures equaling or exceeding established records in several locations. Hot temperatures 4 to 7 degrees above normal were experienced in Cochise County. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for a total of 11 individuals.  Six individuals found in the desert, two on the Tohono O'odham Nation, one in Santa Cruz County, two in southeast Pima County and one in Tucson Mountain Park. Four victims were found in the Tucson (2 indoors and 2 outdoors), and another individual perished in his home in Bowie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot high temperatures of 104 to 110 degrees occurred with low temperatures in the upper 70s to lower 80s, and caused the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Picacho set a record with 110 degrees on the 11th. San Manuel set records with 104 on the 10th and 105 on the 11th, and tied a record with 106 on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 4 to 6 degrees above normal were experienced across Pima County on September 5 and 6. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert on the Tohono O'odham Nation and was a contributing factor for one individual in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot high temperatures 101 to 103 degrees, or 4 to 6 degrees above normal, occurred on September 6 causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was a contributing factor in the death of one individual found in their Marana home. Heart disease was a complicating factor in this fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries originating from distant thunderstorms converged on the valley floor during the evening hours. This combined with a pocket of upper-level divergence as analyzed by the SPC mesoanalysis resulted in the development of a strong thunderstorm over Chandler. Other weaker thunderstorms developed across the valley as well from the resulting outflow boundaries. A strong thunderstorm that developed near Morristown caused downed powerlines, resulting in the closure of US-60. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arizona Department of Transportation camera along State Route 347 near Cement Plant Road showed near zero visibility in dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. In addition, a social media image of a person driving down State Route 347 between W Riggs Road and the Gila River Bridge showed a few hundred feet visibility. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dust devil developed and moved in an urbanized sector of Fortuna Foothills just east of Yuma, causing extensive property damage. There were social media images of roofs torn off, roofing tiles blown off, brick wall collapsing, trees uprooted, and massive debris hitting power lines, knocking power out to 2022 customers. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong dust devil developed and moved northward in an urbanized sector of Fortuna Foothills just east of Yuma, causing extensive property damage. There were social media images of roofs torn off, roofing tiles blown off, a brick wall collapsing, trees uprooted and massive debris hitting power lines, knocking power out to 2022 customers. The dust devil tracked northward between 10E and 36th Street and South Payson Drive to South Fontage Road near I-8. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-2' in less than 1 hour caused the flooding of Magee Rd. east of La Canada Dr. at Pegler Wash and the closure of Ina Rd. at Pima Wash east of Oracle Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona from mid morning through early evening. A couple of storms downed trees and produced flash flooding in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-2 in less than 1 hour caused urban washes to flow over roadways from midtown to southeast Tucson. One person was washed downstream in the Columbus Wash near Blacklidge Dr. but was able to get themselves to safety. A young woman was rescued from her car which was stuck in Arcadia Wash near Pima and Rosemont. Kolb Rd. was closed between Irvington and Valencia while Irvington Rd. was closed due to flooding at the Atterbury Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved erratically across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. In the Tucson Metro, localized flash flooding occurred and a landspout was spotted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout was spotted near the Los Reales landfill on the south side of Tucson. It was nearly stationary before it dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved erratically across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. In the Tucson Metro, localized flash flooding occurred and a landspout was spotted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-1.5 on Tucson's southeast side caused localized flash flooding. Escalante Rd. was closed at the Atterbury Wash and a man was rescued from his vehicle in the Alamo Wash at Yale Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough at 700 mb moved across southern Arizona and with sufficient instability and moisture in place with MLCAPE of around 1000 J/KG and PWAT values of between 1.3 and 1.6 inches, widespread thunderstorm activity was observed across most of south-central and southwest Arizona. Storms first developed across the higher terrain north and east of Phoenix as well as across portions of southern Maricopa County during the early afternoon hours. These storms sent two outflow boundaries from opposing directions, which then collided across the Phoenix metro, sparking the development of additional storms over the Phoenix area. Another cluster of storms then developed across portions of southwest Arizona during the late afternoon into the early evening hours.  No damages or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bouse Fire Department reported washes were running along SR-72 near Bouse, resulting in difficult access as well as the closure of several sections of the highway. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved southwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening.  In the Tucson Metro area, damaging winds downed dozens of power poles and heavy rain resulted in flash flooding. Flash flooding also occurred in the Cochise County community of St. David.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed 38 power poles and numerous trees along Kolb Rd. between Golf Links and Escalante Rds. The road remained closed for more than 24 hours to clean up the area prior to replacing all the poles.  At least 6000 customers were left without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across southeast Arizona during the late afternoon and evening. Storms produced large hail and damaging winds in the Sierra Vista. Heavy rain caused flash flooding in St. David and far eastern Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2-3 in 1 hour east of Saint David caused flash flooding in town. Homes were flooded near Cottonwood and Miller Lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northwest across southeast Arizona from late morning into early evening. Flash flooding occurred near Three Points and in Sabino and Bear Canyons where a large group of people became stranded. A dust storm rolled across Interstate 10 in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2-3 at the headwaters of Sabino Creek and Bear Canyon caused rapid rises in both streams. Flash flooding occurred at the Sabino Canyon Recreation area causing damage to trails and flooding of all recreation area bridges. Twenty five people became stranded in Bear Canyon and needed to be rescued as trails across the wash were flowing heavily and dangerously impassable. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low located over the Great Basin produced cooler than normal 500 mb temperature of between -9 and -10C and also some upper-level diffluence with a 50-60 kt jet at upper-levels. Moisture levels were also above normal with precipitable water values between 1.0-1.3 inches. The combination of all of these factors resulted in sufficient instability with MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/KG while DCAPE values were approaching 1500 J/KG. Thunderstorms began to develop during the early to mid-afternoon hours across most of southern and central Arizona, with strong to locally damaging winds as well as dense blowing dust being the main hazards along with locally heavy rainfall. One of the damaged areas came just northwest of Stanfield, where there were multiple downed power poles as well as a rolled over RV. There were also visible photos from social media as well as from seasoned storm chasers of a couple of landspouts, one near damaged area just northwest of Stanfield and another over Buckeye. With respect to the landspout that developed just northwest of Stanfield, there was no confirmation that it was responsible for the damage observed, which most likely was due to downburst wind. No injuries were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from storm chasers and local media of multiple downed power poles, trees, flipped irrigation pivots, and a rolled RV along and just east of SR-347, northwest of Stanfield. The local utility company reported around 50 downed power poles across the area, most of which were blown toward the north. There was a confirmed landspout in the general area as well, however, there was no evidence that it was the cause of any of the damage. Therefore, the downburst winds are being treated as the official cause of the damages. Winds were estimated at around 70 mph after discussion with the utility company. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the latter part of June, the Southwest was under the influence of a strong ridge of high pressure centered over the southern Plains. This allowed temperatures to climb slightly above seasonal normal values between the 21st through the 26th. At Phoenix Sky Harbor, afternoon high temperatures topped out at 108 degrees for several days during this time. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 13 heat-associated fatalities occurred due to this heat event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three fatalities (1 direct and 2 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low located over the Great Basin produced cooler than normal 500 mb temperature of between -9 and -10C and also some upper-level diffluence with a 50-60 kt jet at upper-levels. Moisture levels were also above normal with precipitable water values between 1.0-1.3 inches. The combination of all of these factors resulted in sufficient instability with MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/KG while DCAPE values were approaching 1500 J/KG. Thunderstorms began to develop during the early to mid-afternoon hours across most of southern and central Arizona, with strong to locally damaging winds as well as dense blowing dust being the main hazards along with locally heavy rainfall. One of the damaged areas came just northwest of Stanfield, where there were multiple downed power poles as well as a rolled over RV. There were also visible photos from social media as well as from seasoned storm chasers of a couple of landspouts, one near damaged area just northwest of Stanfield and another over Buckeye. With respect to the landspout that developed just northwest of Stanfield, there was no confirmation that it was responsible for the damage observed, which most likely was due to downburst wind. No injuries were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than 1/4 mile in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow on I-10 near Casa Grande. No accidents or injuries were reported.
